statement: Human papillomavirus (HPV) is the most common sexually transmitted infection globally, and HPV types 16 and 18 cause approximately 70% of cervical cancers.
quizQuestion: Which two HPV types cause approximately 70% of cervical cancers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, HPV (human papillomavirus) is the most common sexually transmitted infection globally. HPV 16 and HPV 18 are the most carcinogenic strains, responsible for approximately 70% of all cervical cancer cases worldwide.
wowFactor: 70% of cervical cancer worldwide is caused by just two strains of HPV — making HPV vaccination one of the most effective cancer-prevention tools ever developed!

variants.question: True or false: HPV is the most common sexually transmitted infection in the world.

statement: Scarlet fever was one of the leading causes of childhood death in the early 20th century but is now easily treated with antibiotics.
quizQuestion: What antibiotic is now used to easily treat scarlet fever that once killed many children?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, scarlet fever was a leading cause of childhood death in the early 20th century. The disease is caused by Streptococcus pyogenes and is now easily treated with penicillin — a stunning reversal made possible by antibiotics.
wowFactor: Scarlet fever once killed thousands of children every year. Today, a 10-day course of penicillin cures it completely — one of medicine's most dramatic turnarounds!

variants.question: True or false: scarlet fever is caused by Streptococcus pyogenes bacteria.

statement: There are approximately 535,000 cardiac arrests in the United States every year — 61% occurring outside hospitals, where survival rates are dramatically lower.
quizQuestion: What percentage of US cardiac arrests occur outside of a hospital?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, per the 2015 American Heart Association Guidelines, there were approximately 535,000 cardiac arrests annually in the US. Of these, 326,000 (61%) occurred outside of a hospital setting, where survival is far less likely.
wowFactor: 61% of cardiac arrests happen outside hospitals — on sidewalks, in homes, in cars. That's why CPR training saves lives: you are far more likely to need it than a doctor is.

variants.question: True or false: the majority of US cardiac arrests occur inside hospitals.

statement: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death worldwide — except in Africa — causing 17.9 million deaths in 2015, or 32.1% of all deaths globally.
quizQuestion: Cardiovascular disease is the world's leading cause of death — on every continent except which one?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cardiovascular diseases are the leading cause of death worldwide except Africa. Together CVD resulted in 17.9 million deaths (32.1%) in 2015, up from 12.3 million (25.8%) in 1990.
wowFactor: Heart disease kills 32% of everyone who dies on Earth — on every continent except Africa, where different disease burdens dominate. It's the closest thing to a universal cause of death humanity has.

variants.question: True or false: cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death on every continent including Africa.

statement: Arteries carry oxygenated blood away from the heart — except the pulmonary arteries, which carry deoxygenated blood TO the lungs.
quizQuestion: Which arteries are the exception to the rule that arteries carry oxygenated blood?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, arteries generally carry oxygenated blood away from the heart, but two exceptions exist: the pulmonary arteries (carrying deoxygenated blood to the lungs) and the umbilical arteries in fetal circulation (carrying deoxygenated blood to the placenta).
wowFactor: The rule 'arteries carry oxygen, veins carry carbon dioxide' has a major exception — the pulmonary arteries go in reverse. This trips up nursing students and trivia enthusiasts alike.

variants.question: True or false: all arteries in the human body carry oxygenated blood.

variants.correctAnswer: Arteries carry blood away from the heart

statement: Anemia is the most common blood disorder in the world, affecting a fifth to a third of the global population — iron-deficiency anemia alone affects nearly one billion people.
quizQuestion: How many people worldwide are affected by iron-deficiency anemia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, anemia is the most common blood disorder, affecting about a fifth to a third of the global population. Iron-deficiency anemia is the most common cause worldwide, affecting nearly one billion people.
wowFactor: Nearly one billion people — about 1 in 8 humans alive right now — have iron-deficiency anemia. It's the world's most common nutritional disorder, yet it's largely fixable with diet or cheap supplements.

variants.question: True or false: iron-deficiency anemia is the most common form of anemia worldwide.

statement: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels in the body — just 5 to 10 micrometres wide, thinner than a human hair — and are the only vessels where gas exchange with body tissues occurs.
quizQuestion: What is the diameter range of capillaries, the body's smallest blood vessels?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, capillaries are 5 to 10 micrometres in diameter and are composed of only a single thin wall of squamous endothelial cells. They are where oxygen, carbon dioxide, glucose, and other substances are exchanged with surrounding tissues.
wowFactor: A human hair is roughly 70 micrometres thick — capillaries are 7-14 times thinner. These impossibly tiny tubes are where every cell in your body receives its oxygen and disposes of its waste.

variants.question: True or false: capillaries are wider than arterioles.

visualDescription: An extreme magnification pixel-art view of a single capillary — barely wider than the red blood cells squeezing single-file through it — oxygen bubbles passing through the hair-thin wall into surrounding tissue.

statement: Only 5 to 10 percent of breast cancer cases result from an inherited genetic predisposition — the vast majority are caused by lifestyle and environmental factors.
quizQuestion: What percentage of breast cancer cases are caused by inherited genetic mutations like BRCA?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, about five to ten percent of breast cancer cases are the result of an inherited genetic predisposition, including BRCA mutations. The remaining 90-95% are caused by lifestyle and environmental risk factors.
wowFactor: Most people assume breast cancer is mainly genetic — but inherited mutations like BRCA cause only 5-10% of cases. The other 90%+ come from lifestyle and environment, meaning most breast cancer is actually preventable.

variants.question: True or false: the majority of breast cancer cases are due to inherited gene mutations.

statement: One in eight men will be diagnosed with prostate cancer in their lifetime, making it the second-leading cause of cancer death in men.
quizQuestion: What fraction of men will be diagnosed with prostate cancer during their lifetime?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, one in eight men are diagnosed with prostate cancer in their lifetime and one in forty die of the disease. Each year 1.2 million cases are diagnosed and 350,000 die, making it the second-leading cause of cancer death in men.
wowFactor: One in eight men will get prostate cancer — yet most tumors are so slow-growing they never cause harm and require only 'watchful waiting.' The real danger is the small fraction that spreads.

statement: Colorectal cancer is the world's third-most common cancer, with 1.93 million new cases and nearly 904,000 deaths in 2022.
quizQuestion: Where does colorectal cancer rank among all cancer types globally?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, colorectal cancer is globally the third-most common type of cancer, making up about 10% of all cases. In 2022, there were 1.93 million new cases and 903,859 deaths from the disease.
wowFactor: Colorectal cancer kills nearly a million people a year — yet colonoscopy screening can detect and remove the polyps that become cancer before they ever turn dangerous. It's one of the most preventable cancers with a single procedure.

statement: Worldwide rates of HSV-1 or HSV-2 herpes infection are between 60 and 95 percent in adults — meaning most people carry the virus without knowing it.
quizQuestion: What percentage range of adults worldwide carry herpes (HSV-1 or HSV-2)?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, worldwide rates of either HSV-1 or HSV-2 are between 60% and 95% in adults. HSV-1 is usually acquired during childhood and since there is no cure, rates inherently increase as people age.
wowFactor: Up to 95% of adults carry herpes. It's so common that 'having herpes' is almost the default — yet it's so stigmatized most people don't know they're infected or that their partners likely are too.

statement: The vertebrate circulatory system is a closed system — blood never leaves blood vessels. Many invertebrates like arthropods have open systems where blood circulates freely in body cavities.
quizQuestion: What makes the vertebrate circulatory system 'closed' compared to arthropods?
distractors: Blood flows in only one direction
The heart is enclosed in bone
Blood is sealed in the heart
All blood flows in loops only
acceptableAnswers: blood never leaves the blood vessels
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the circulatory system in vertebrates is closed — blood never leaves the network of blood vessels. Many invertebrates such as arthropods have an open circulatory system where the blood (hemolymph) pumped by the heart returns via body cavities rather than vessels.
wowFactor: Insects don't have blood vessels — their 'blood' (hemolymph) just sloshes around inside their bodies. You have a tightly sealed pipe network; a fly has an open swimming pool.

statement: An estimated 33 to 106 million new gonorrhea cases occur each year, and resistance to previously used antibiotics has been increasing.
quizQuestion: What is the estimated range of new gonorrhea cases per year globally?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an estimated 33 to 106 million new gonorrhea cases occur each year globally. Resistance has developed to many previously used antibiotics, making treatment with ceftriaxone by injection and azithromycin the current standard.
wowFactor: Gonorrhea is rapidly becoming untreatable — antibiotic-resistant 'super-gonorrhea' has been spreading for years. An infection that used to be cured with penicillin now requires injections of last-resort antibiotics.

statement: The WHO estimates that smoking killed over 7 million smokers in 2023, plus 1.3 million non-smokers killed by second-hand smoke.
quizQuestion: How many non-smokers does second-hand smoke kill per year, according to the WHO?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the WHO estimated that smoking killed over seven million smokers worldwide in 2023, and an additional 1.3 million non-smokers due to second-hand smoke.
wowFactor: Second-hand smoke kills 1.3 million people who chose never to smoke. You can decide not to smoke — but you can't always decide what air you breathe.

statement: Skin infections represent more than 95% of all human anthrax cases — the rarest and most deadly form is inhalation anthrax.
quizQuestion: What is the term for a accounts for more than 95% of all human cases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, skin infections represent more than 95% of anthrax cases. Respiratory anthrax (inhalation form) has a mortality of 50–80% even with treatment, making it far deadlier — which is why it has been developed as a bioweapon.
wowFactor: Anthrax as a bioweapon targets the lungs — the form with 50-80% mortality. But 95% of natural anthrax cases are just skin infections with only a 23.7% untreated death rate. The bioterror version and the natural version are almost different diseases.

The returning pulmonary veins then carry oxygenated blood back to the heart.

statement: Typhoid fever is caused by the bacterium Salmonella Typhi, which is believed to infect and replicate only in humans — there are no known animal reservoirs.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. Unusually, Salmonella Typhi is believed to infect and replicate only within humans — there are no known animal reservoirs. This differs from other Salmonella species which cause food poisoning in many animals. Typhoid spreads via food or water contaminated with faeces of infected people. In 2015, 12.5 million new cases were reported worldwide.
wowFactor: Unlike most infectious diseases that hide in animals, typhoid's bacteria survive exclusively in humans — meaning humans themselves are the only known reservoir, making humans both host and spreader!

statement: Liver cirrhosis replaces healthy liver tissue with scar tissue (fibrosis), causing portal hypertension and potentially fatal complications — yet no specific treatment exists; the only definitive cure is a liver transplant.
quizQuestion: What is the only definitive cure for advanced cirrhosis?
distractors: Stem cell injection into the liver
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cirrhosis is a chronic condition where normal liver tissue is replaced by scar tissue (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules. This causes increased resistance to blood flow through the liver (portal hypertension), and can lead to liver cancer, encephalopathy, and bleeding. No specific treatment for cirrhosis itself exists — management focuses on treating underlying causes. The only definitive cure for end-stage disease is liver transplantation.
wowFactor: Cirrhosis scars the most versatile organ in your body — permanently replacing working tissue with scar — and there is currently no drug that can reverse it. A transplant is the only way out.

statement: Cardiac arrest and heart attack are often confused: a heart attack is a plumbing problem (blocked artery), while cardiac arrest is an electrical problem (the heart stops beating entirely).
quizQuestion: What is the fundamental difference between a heart attack and cardiac arrest?
correctAnswer: A heart attack is a blocked artery; cardiac arrest is the heart stopping beating
distractors: A heart attack is more severe than cardiac arrest
Cardiac arrest only happens in the elderly
They are the same condition with different names
A heart attack always leads to cardiac arrest
Cardiac arrest is caused by blocked arteries too
Only cardiac arrest can be treated with CPR
Heart attacks are always fatal; cardiac arrest is treatable
Cardiac arrest only occurs in athletes
acceptableAnswers: heart attack is blocked artery, cardiac arrest is electrical/heart stops
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cardiac arrest is a condition in which the heart suddenly and unexpectedly stops beating due to an arrhythmia (electrical problem). A heart attack (myocardial infarction) is caused by a blocked coronary artery (plumbing problem) cutting off blood supply to heart muscle. A heart attack can trigger cardiac arrest, but they are distinct conditions. Approximately 535,000 incidents of cardiac arrest occur annually in the US.
wowFactor: Most people think 'heart attack' and 'cardiac arrest' mean the same thing — they don't! One is your heart's plumbing failing, the other is the electrical system crashing. Knowing the difference could save a life!

statement: Osteoarthritis — the most common form of arthritis — occurs when cartilage wears away causing bone to grind on bone; it affects more than 3.8% of people globally and is not an autoimmune disease.
quizQuestion: What is the most common form of arthritis worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, arthritis describes disorders where cartilage lining a joint is lost, causing bone-on-bone grinding during movement. The most common form is osteoarthritis, affecting more than 3.8% of people globally — it typically occurs with age and affects weight-bearing joints like hips, knees, and fingers. Rheumatoid arthritis, the second most common form (0.24% prevalence), is an autoimmune disorder. Osteoarthritis is not autoimmune.
wowFactor: Arthritis is not one disease — it is over 100 conditions! And the most common type, osteoarthritis, is NOT an autoimmune disease; it is simply cartilage wearing down like old brakes on a car.

statement: Scabies — historically known as the 'seven-year itch' — is caused by a mite just 0.2–0.45 mm in size that burrows into skin to lay eggs, and typically only 10–15 mites are involved in an entire infection.

distractors: A bacterial infection in hair follicles

A parasitic worm living in skin layers
A yeast overgrowth on the skin
explanation: According to Wikipedia, scabies is caused by infestation with the tiny mite Sarcoptes scabiei variety hominis, which is just 0.2–0.45 mm in size. The female mites burrow into the skin to live and deposit eggs. The characteristic intense itch is due to an allergic reaction to the mites. Remarkably, typically only 10–15 mites are involved in an entire infection. It spreads through prolonged direct skin contact, at least 10 minutes.
wowFactor: The dreaded 'seven-year itch' of history is caused by a mite barely visible to the naked eye — and an entire scabies infection typically involves fewer than 15 mites total!

statement: Neanderthal males had a brain volume averaging 1,640 cc — larger than the average of any living human population — yet they interbred with modern Homo sapiens, and most non-African people carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA today.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, Neanderthal brain volume averaged 1,640 cc for males and 1,460 cc for females — higher than any living human population. However, their brain organisation differed from modern humans in areas related to cognition and language. Neanderthals went extinct around 40,000 years ago, but genetic sequencing (first in 2010) confirmed they interbred with Homo sapiens — most non-African people today carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA.
wowFactor: Neanderthals had bigger brains than us — on average. Yet they went extinct while we survived. And we literally carry part of their genes in most people alive today — so they never truly disappeared!

statement: Humans perceive five basic tastes — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami — but cats cannot taste sweetness at all, and dolphins and sea lions have lost the ability to sense up to four of their ancestral five basic tastes.
quizQuestion: How many basic tastes can humans perceive?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the five basic tastes are sweetness, sourness, saltiness, bitterness, and savoriness (umami). Scientific experiments have demonstrated that all five are distinct. Taste buds contain 50–100 receptor cells each, and humans have 2,000–5,000 taste buds. Notably, cats cannot taste sweetness, and several carnivores including dolphins and sea lions have lost up to four of their ancestral five basic tastes through evolution.
wowFactor: Cats literally cannot taste sweet things — they evolved no sweetness receptors because they are pure carnivores. And dolphins have lost most of their tastes entirely, probably because they swallow fish whole!

statement: Digestion begins in the mouth: saliva contains the enzyme salivary amylase, which hydrolyses roughly 30% of dietary starch into disaccharides before the food even reaches the stomach.
quizQuestion: What percentage of dietary starch does salivary amylase break down in the mouth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, saliva secreted by the salivary glands contains salivary amylase, which begins the chemical digestion of starch in food. About 30% of starch is hydrolysed into disaccharides in the oral cavity. Saliva also contains mucus for lubrication and bicarbonate to provide ideal pH conditions for amylase activity. After swallowing, the food travels through the oesophagus by peristalsis to the stomach, where protein digestion begins.
wowFactor: Digestion doesn't start in your stomach — it starts the moment food touches your saliva! Your mouth is already a chemical laboratory, breaking down starch before you even swallow.

Diseases related to tobacco have been shown to kill approximately half of long-term smokers compared to non-smokers.

statement: Between 60% and 95% of adults worldwide carry herpes simplex virus (HSV-1 or HSV-2) — and once infected, the virus remains in nerve cells for life, periodically reactivating.
quizQuestion: What percentage of adults worldwide carry herpes simplex virus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, worldwide rates of either HSV-1 or HSV-2 infection are between 60% and 95% in adults. HSV-1 (typically causing oral herpes/cold sores) is usually acquired during childhood. After infection, the virus travels along sensory nerves to nerve cell bodies where it resides lifelong. There is no cure for either type; antiviral medications like aciclovir can reduce the severity of outbreaks.
wowFactor: Herpes simplex infects the majority of adults worldwide — up to 95% in some populations — making it arguably the most widespread persistent viral infection in humanity. Yet most people never know they have it!

statement: Lichens — composites of fungi, algae or cyanobacteria, and bacteria — were the lifeform that first introduced the word 'symbiosis' into biological usage, and some are among the oldest living things on Earth.
quizQuestion: Which lifeform is credited with introducing the concept of symbiosis into biology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lichens are hybrid colonies of algae or cyanobacteria living symbiotically among filaments of multiple fungus species, also hosting bacteria. Lichens are the lifeform that first brought the term symbiosis (as Symbiotismus) into biological context. There are about 20,000 known lichen species. Some are among the oldest living things, and lichenometry — measuring their slow and regular growth rate — is used to date geological events.
wowFactor: The scientific concept of 'symbiosis' — two organisms living together for mutual benefit — was coined specifically to describe lichens. That grey crust on rocks is literally the organism that invented the word for cooperation!

variants.question: True or false: lichens are a type of plant.

statement: The three-domain system of life — Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukarya — was proposed in 1990 by Carl Woese, Otto Kandler, and Mark Wheelis, replacing the older two-kingdom model and revealing that archaea are more closely related to us than to bacteria.
quizQuestion: What are the three domains in Carl Woese's three-domain system of life?
distractors: DNA, RNA, and Protein life forms
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the three-domain system was introduced in 1990 by Carl Woese, Otto Kandler, and Mark Wheelis. The three domains are Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukarya (which includes all plants, animals, fungi, and protists). Prior to this system, life was divided into two groups — prokaryotes and eukaryotes. The discovery revealed that archaea, despite being single-celled and lacking nuclei, are more closely related to eukaryotes (including humans) than to bacteria.
wowFactor: Scientists used to think life was simply bacteria vs. everything else — then Woese discovered that one group of bacteria-like microbes (archaea) is actually more closely related to YOU than to normal bacteria. The family tree of life was redrawn overnight!

variants.question: True or false: archaea are more closely related to humans (eukaryotes) than they are to bacteria.

statement: The World Health Organization maintains a list of essential medicines considered necessary for all functioning health systems worldwide.
quizQuestion: Which global body publishes a list of essential medicines for all health systems?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the World Health Organization keeps a list of essential medicines — drugs considered minimum requirements for a basic functioning health system. Countries use this list to prioritize which medicines to make universally available.
wowFactor: There's an official WHO shortlist of the medicines every country in the world is supposed to have — a kind of universal pharmacy floor.

statement: Drugs are classified by level of control into prescription drugs, dispensed only by a pharmacist on a doctor's order, and over-the-counter drugs that consumers can obtain freely.
quizQuestion: What key division separates prescription drugs from over-the-counter drugs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, one of the key divisions in drug classification is by level of control — prescription drugs require a pharmacist to dispense them only on a medical prescription, while over-the-counter drugs can be ordered by consumers for themselves without a doctor's note.
wowFactor: The entire prescription vs. over-the-counter split isn't about how dangerous a drug is chemically — it's about who controls access to it.

statement: Ebola has a mortality rate ranging from 25% to 90%, averaging approximately 50% — making it one of the deadliest known infectious diseases.
quizQuestion: What is the average mortality rate of Ebola disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Ebola virus disease causes a mortality rate of anywhere between 25 and 90%, averaging out at approximately 50%. Death is often due to shock from fluid loss, typically occurring between 6 and 16 days after the first symptoms.
wowFactor: Flip a coin — that's essentially your survival odds with Ebola. It's one of the few modern diseases where half of all patients die.

statement: Fruit bats can carry and spread Ebola virus without being harmed by it, making them the natural reservoir of the disease.
quizQuestion: Which animal can spread Ebola without being harmed by the virus itself?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fruit bats are believed to be the natural host of the Ebola viruses — they are able to spread the viruses without being affected by them. The virus first jumped to humans through contact with infected wildlife, especially in sub-Saharan Africa.
wowFactor: Fruit bats carry one of the deadliest viruses on Earth and feel absolutely nothing — they're living biohazard containers that never get sick.

statement: Ebola symptoms can appear anywhere from 2 to 21 days after infection — a 3-week window that makes outbreak containment extremely difficult.
quizQuestion: What is the maximum incubation period for Ebola in humans?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Ebola has a minimal incubation period of 2 days and a maximal incubation period of 21 days in humans. This wide window means infected people can unknowingly travel or interact with others long before symptoms appear, complicating quarantine efforts.
wowFactor: An infected person could feel totally fine for three weeks while silently spreading Ebola — which is exactly what makes outbreak containment so nightmarish.

statement: In 2015, approximately 1.1 billion people worldwide had sexually transmitted infections other than HIV/AIDS.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, globally in 2015, about 1.1 billion people had STIs other than HIV/AIDS. About 500 million had either syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia, or trichomoniasis — four of the most common curable infections.
wowFactor: One in seven people alive in 2015 had a sexually transmitted infection — making STIs among the most prevalent diseases on Earth.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, historical documentation of STIs in antiquity dates back to at least the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) and the Hebrew Bible/Old Testament (8th/7th century BCE). Sexually transmitted infections are among humanity's oldest documented diseases.
wowFactor: Ancient Egyptians were writing medical notes about STIs 3,500 years ago — this is not a modern problem.

statement: As of 2023, only Pakistan and Afghanistan remain endemic for wild poliovirus — down from 350,000 cases globally in 1988.
quizQuestion: As of October 2023, which two countries still have wild poliovirus transmission?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of October 2023, only Pakistan and Afghanistan remain endemic for wild poliovirus. Through vaccination, global cases dropped from an estimated 350,000 in 1988 to just 30 confirmed cases in 2022 — one of history's greatest public health achievements.
wowFactor: In 1988 polio paralyzed 350,000 children a year. By 2022 it was down to 30 cases — confined to just two countries. Vaccines did that.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, approximately 75% of polio cases are asymptomatic. This is what makes eradication so hard — most infected people feel fine but can still spread the virus through fecal-oral transmission for up to six weeks.
wowFactor: Three out of four people who catch polio feel completely fine — yet can silently spread it to others for six weeks.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, years after recovery from polio, post-polio syndrome may occur — with a slow development of muscle weakness similar to what the person had during the initial infection. It's a late complication that can emerge decades after the original illness.
wowFactor: Polio can come back to haunt you decades later — survivors may suddenly develop new muscle weakness long after they thought they'd fully recovered.

wowFactor: Despite vaccines and antibiotics, infection still kills nearly 1 in 6 people worldwide — it's not a solved problem.

wowFactor: Taking an antibiotic for a viral infection does literally nothing — each pathogen type needs its own completely different weapon.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, cancer was first found in humans in 3000 BCE in Egypt. The Edwin Smith Papyrus described a form of cancer as 'a bulging tumor of the breast.' This makes cancer one of the oldest documented diseases in human history.

statement: The term 'epidemiology' was first used to describe the study of epidemics in 1802 by Spanish physician Joaquín de Villalba.
quizQuestion: Who first used the term 'epidemiology' in 1802?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'epidemiology' appears to have first been used to describe the study of epidemics in 1802 by the Spanish physician Joaquín de Villalba in his work 'Epidemiología Española.' The distinction between epidemic and endemic disease was first drawn much earlier by Hippocrates.
wowFactor: A little-known Spanish doctor coined the word 'epidemiology' over 200 years ago — yet we use it every day in pandemic news coverage.

statement: Epidemiologists study the interaction of multiple diseases in a population simultaneously — a condition known as a syndemic.
quizQuestion: What term describes the simultaneous interaction of two or more diseases in a population?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, epidemiologists also study the interaction of diseases in a population — a condition known as a syndemic. Unlike a pandemic (one disease spreading widely), a syndemic describes how multiple diseases co-occur and interact to worsen overall health outcomes.
wowFactor: There's a word for when multiple diseases attack a population at once and make each other worse — 'syndemic' — and it's increasingly relevant to how modern public health works.

statement: The human esophagus is about 25 cm long and carries food from the throat to the stomach entirely by muscular contractions called peristalsis — not gravity.
quizQuestion: What muscular movement propels food down the esophagus to the stomach?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the esophagus is a fibromuscular tube about 25 cm long in adult humans that propels food by peristaltic contractions — waves of muscular movement. This means astronauts can swallow food in zero gravity because peristalsis, not gravity, moves food to the stomach.
wowFactor: You can swallow food while standing on your head — the esophagus uses muscular waves, not gravity, to push everything down!

statement: The human gastrointestinal tract is about 9 meters long at autopsy — but considerably shorter in a living person because the intestines maintain constant muscle tone.
quizQuestion: How long is the human gastrointestinal tract at autopsy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the whole human GI tract is about nine meters (30 feet) long at autopsy. It is considerably shorter in the living body because the intestines, tubes of smooth muscle tissue, maintain constant muscle tone in a halfway-tense state but can relax to allow local distension and peristalsis.
wowFactor: The tube running from your mouth to the exit is 9 meters long — that's nearly three times your height, folded up inside you!

statement: Digestion of starch begins in the mouth: saliva contains salivary amylase, which breaks down about 30% of dietary starch before you even swallow.
quizQuestion: Which enzyme in saliva begins breaking down starch in the mouth before swallowing?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, saliva contains salivary amylase, an enzyme which starts the digestion of starch in the food. About 30% of starch is hydrolyzed into disaccharide in the oral cavity (mouth). This means chewing bread or rice longer makes it noticeably sweeter as amylase releases sugar.
wowFactor: If you chew bread long enough, it starts tasting sweet — that's your mouth literally digesting the starch into sugar in real time!

statement: The citric acid cycle's intermediates are present in all known living organisms — from bacteria to elephants — reflecting its ancient evolutionary origin.
quizQuestion: Which metabolic recurring process's intermediates are found in every known living organism on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the set of carboxylic acids best known as intermediates in the citric acid cycle are present in all known organisms — from the bacterium E. coli to elephants. These similarities are likely due to the pathway's early appearance in evolutionary history and its continued efficacy.
wowFactor: The energy chemistry running in your cells right now is the exact same process running in every bacterium on Earth — a 4-billion-year-old recipe!

statement: The pulse felt at the wrist or neck is the rhythmic expansion of an artery in direct response to each heartbeat — not blood flowing past.
quizQuestion: What physical change in an artery creates the pulse you feel at the wrist or neck?
distractors: Nerve impulse traveling up the arm
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the pulse is the rhythmic expansion and contraction of an artery in response to the cardiac cycle (heartbeat). It can be felt anywhere that an artery is compressed near the surface of the body — neck (carotid), wrist (radial or ulnar), groin (femoral), and others.
wowFactor: Your pulse isn't blood flowing by — it's the artery itself expanding and snapping back like a tiny elastic tube with every heartbeat!

statement: There are five main hepatitis viruses (A, B, C, D, and E), each with different transmission routes — hepatitis D can only infect people already infected with hepatitis B.
quizQuestion: Which liver disease virus can only infect someone who already has the B variant of that infection?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hepatitis D can only infect people already infected with hepatitis B — it requires the hepatitis B surface antigen to replicate. This makes it a 'satellite virus' that depends entirely on another virus. There are vaccines against A, B, and D (via B vaccination), but no vaccine exists yet for hepatitis C.
wowFactor: Hepatitis D is a parasite of a parasite — it can only infect you if hepatitis B already has, making it a virus that needs another virus as its key!

statement: Russian biologist Ilya Mechnikov discovered phagocytosis — the process by which immune cells engulf and destroy foreign invaders — by observing starfish larvae with thorns.
quizQuestion: What immune process did Ilya Mechnikov discover by observing starfish larvae with thorns inserted in them?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, it was Mechnikov who first observed the phenomenon of phagocytosis, in which the body defends itself against a foreign body. He pinned small thorns into starfish larvae and noticed unusual cells surrounding and engulfing the thorns. He received the Nobel Prize in 1908 with Paul Ehrlich for this work on immunity.
wowFactor: The discovery that your immune cells eat invaders alive came from a Russian scientist poking thorns into starfish — science is beautifully weird!

statement: In liver cirrhosis, normal liver tissue is progressively replaced by scar tissue (fibrosis), eventually blocking blood flow and causing the liver to fail.
quizQuestion: In liver cirrhosis, what replaces normal functioning liver over time?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cirrhosis is a chronic liver condition in which normal functioning tissue (parenchyma) is replaced with scar tissue (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules. This causes increased resistance to blood flow in hepatic sinusoids and consequently portal hypertension, eventually leading to liver failure.
wowFactor: Your liver tries to heal itself from injury by scarring — but enough scars and the repair mechanism itself destroys the organ!

correctAnswer: It has developed resistance to many antibiotics

explanation: According to Wikipedia, gonorrhea treatment is usually with ceftriaxone by injection and azithromycin by mouth; however, resistance has developed to many previously used antibiotics and higher doses of ceftriaxone are occasionally required. The WHO lists gonorrhea as one of the priority pathogens for new antibiotic development.
wowFactor: Gonorrhea has outsmarted almost every antibiotic we've thrown at it — it's becoming one of the first untreatable common infections in history!

statement: An aneurysm is a balloon-like bulge in a blood vessel wall caused by a localized weakness — and as it grows, the risk of a catastrophic rupture increases.
quizQuestion: What happens to rupture risk as an aneurysm grows larger?
distractors: Risk reverses after a certain size
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an aneurysm is an outward bulging caused by a localized, abnormal, weak spot on a blood vessel wall. As an aneurysm increases in size, the risk of rupture increases — which could lead to uncontrolled bleeding. Particularly dangerous examples include aortic aneurysms and brain aneurysms in the circle of Willis.
wowFactor: An aneurysm is a ticking time bomb inside a blood vessel — the bigger it gets, the closer it is to potentially fatal rupture!

variants.question: True or false: the risk of an aneurysm rupturing increases as the aneurysm grows in size.

statement: Congenital insensitivity to pain — being born unable to feel pain — may reduce life expectancy because pain serves as the body's critical warning system.
quizQuestion: Why can being born unable to feel actually shorten your life?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, pain motivates organisms to withdraw from damaging situations, to protect a damaged body part while it heals, and to avoid similar experiences in the future. Congenital insensitivity to pain may result in reduced life expectancy because without pain, serious injuries, infections, and damage go unnoticed until they become fatal.
wowFactor: Never feeling pain sounds amazing — until you realize people born without it often die young because their body can't warn them when something is seriously wrong!

variants.question: True or false: congenital insensitivity to pain is associated with reduced life expectancy.

variants.correctAnswer: Injuries and damage go undetected and untreated
distractors: They feel cold instead of pain

statement: Scabies is caused by a mite only 0.2–0.45 mm in size — invisible to the naked eye — that burrows into skin to lay eggs, causing intense itching.
quizQuestion: What organism causes scabies by burrowing under human skin to lay eggs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, scabies is caused by infection with the female mite Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis, which is 0.2–0.45 mm in size — at the limit of detection with the human eye. The symptoms of scabies are due to an allergic reaction to the mites rather than the burrowing itself. Often, only 10–15 mites are involved in an infection.
wowFactor: Scabies is caused by a creature almost too small to see — typically just 10 to 15 mites can make an entire person itch uncontrollably!

statement: German scientist Robert Koch was the first person to identify Bacillus anthracis as the bacterium that causes anthrax — a landmark moment for germ theory.
quizQuestion: Which German scientist first identified Bacillus anthracis as the cause of anthrax?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the German scientist Robert Koch was the first to identify Bacillus anthracis as the bacterium that causes anthrax. This was one of Koch's most important early contributions to microbiology and helped establish the germ theory of disease. Koch later won the Nobel Prize for his work on tuberculosis.
wowFactor: Robert Koch identified the anthrax bacterium in 1877 — proving that invisible germs cause disease at a time when most doctors still blamed 'bad air'!

statement: Capsaicin in chili peppers triggers the same pain receptors that detect actual heat, which is why spicy food feels literally burning hot.
quizQuestion: What compound in chili peppers activates the body's heat-sensing pain receptors?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, capsaicin and related capsaicinoids give chili peppers their pungency when ingested or applied topically. They work by binding to the TRPV1 receptor — the same receptor that fires when tissue is exposed to damaging heat — which is why the burning sensation from capsaicin and actual fire feel neurologically identical.
wowFactor: Your mouth isn't being tricked — capsaicin literally hijacks the same nerve signal your body uses for real burns!

variants.question: Which spice compound is also used topically for pain relief by overwhelming pain receptors?

statement: Kangaroos replace their worn-down molars by growing new teeth that migrate forward from the back of the jaw — a process shared among mammals only by elephants and manatees.
quizQuestion: Which mammals besides kangaroos replace teeth by growing new ones that move forward through the jaw?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, kangaroo molars are ground down by abrasive silica in grass, then physically move forward in the mouth before falling out — replaced by new teeth growing in from the back. This process, called polyphyodonty, occurs among mammals only in kangaroos, elephants, and manatees, making it an extraordinarily rare dental adaptation.
wowFactor: Kangaroos, elephants, and manatees share a secret: instead of just two sets of teeth like us, they run a conveyor belt of molars through their jaws!

variants.question: True or false: kangaroos, elephants, and manatees are the only mammals that grow replacement teeth migrating forward through the jaw.

variants.correctAnswer: Abrasive silica in grass wears them down
distractors: High sugar in their diet rots them

statement: Woolly mammoths survived on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago — when the Egyptian pyramids and Mesopotamian civilizations already existed.
quizQuestion: How recently did woolly mammoths survive on Russia's Wrangel Island?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, woolly mammoths (Mammuthus primigenius) on Wrangel Island survived until about 4,000 years ago — long after the first Egyptian pyramids were built and while ancient Mesopotamian civilizations flourished. This close overlap with human history makes mammoth DNA recovery and genetic study particularly significant for science.
wowFactor: Woolly mammoths were still alive when ancient Egyptians were building their civilization — and their intact DNA from permafrost now drives real de-extinction research!

variants.question: True or false: woolly mammoths on Wrangel Island survived long enough to co-exist with ancient Egyptian civilization.

statement: Eyes with resolving power have evolved independently in ten fundamentally different forms across the animal kingdom.
quizQuestion: How many fundamentally different structural forms have eyes with resolving power evolved into?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, eyes with resolving power have come in ten fundamentally different forms, classified broadly into compound eyes (made of many small visual units, common in insects and crustaceans) and non-compound eyes (single lens focusing onto a retina, common in mammals). This remarkable convergent evolution shows that vision is such a survival advantage it has been independently invented by evolution at least ten times.
wowFactor: Vision is so useful that evolution invented the eye not once, not twice, but at least ten completely different ways — each one an independent reinvention of sight!

statement: The eye does more than see: specialized retinal ganglion cells detect light to regulate the body's circadian clock, independently of vision.
quizQuestion: What body clock function do the eyes control that does NOT require conscious vision?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, retinal photosensitive ganglion cells send signals along the retinohypothalamic tract to the suprachiasmatic nuclei to effect circadian adjustment — and separately control the pupillary light reflex. These functions are entirely independent of the visual image-forming pathway, meaning blind individuals can still have light-regulated body clocks if these cells are intact.
wowFactor: Even people who are completely blind can still have their internal clock set by light — because the eye has a secret second job that has nothing to do with seeing images!

variants.question: True or false: some people who are visually blind can still have their circadian rhythm regulated by light through the eyes.

statement: The human cerebellum contains an estimated 55–70 billion neurons — roughly four times more than the cerebral cortex's 14–16 billion.
quizQuestion: Which brain region contains far more neurons than the cerebral cortex — roughly 55 to 70 billion?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the human cerebral cortex contains approximately 14–16 billion neurons, while the cerebellum contains an estimated 55–70 billion — making the cerebellum by far the most neuron-dense brain structure. Despite this, the cerebellum is primarily responsible for motor coordination and balance, not higher cognition.
wowFactor: The part of your brain that coordinates balance and movement has four times more neurons than the 'thinking' cortex — your inner athlete outweighs your inner Einstein!

statement: The prefrontal cortex, which controls executive functions such as planning and decision-making, is particularly well developed in humans compared to other animals.
quizQuestion: Which brain region — controlling planning and decision-making — is unusually enlarged in humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the prefrontal cortex controls executive functions and is particularly well developed in humans. Executive functions include planning, impulse control, and complex decision-making — abilities that distinguish human cognition from most other animals and are believed to underlie our capacity for long-term reasoning and social behavior.
wowFactor: The part of your brain that stops you from making impulsive decisions is uniquely oversized in humans — it's essentially what makes us 'us' compared to other animals!

statement: All vertebrate brains develop from three embryonic divisions: the forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.
quizQuestion: How many primary embryonic does every vertebrate brain develop from?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all vertebrate brains can be embryonically divided into three parts: the forebrain (prosencephalon), midbrain (mesencephalon), and hindbrain (rhombencephalon). This three-part embryonic blueprint is conserved across all vertebrates — from fish to humans — making it one of the oldest architectural plans in animal evolution.
wowFactor: Every vertebrate brain — from a goldfish to a human — starts life as the same three-lump blueprint, a design so successful evolution has kept it for 500 million years!

statement: Neurons send signals as electrochemical impulses along axons, which can trigger electrical synapses or release chemicals called neurotransmitters.
quizQuestion: What chemical messengers do neurons release at synapses to signal other cells?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurons transmit information as electrochemical impulses along axons. At chemical synapses, they release neurotransmitters, which excite, inhibit, or modulate the receiving cell. This allows neural networks to process everything from heartbeat to abstract thought.
wowFactor: Every thought you've ever had is just a cascade of tiny chemical squirts between neurons — consciousness reduced to molecular handshakes!

statement: Sponges, placozoans, and mesozoans are the only multicellular animals with no nervous system whatsoever.
quizQuestion: Which multicellular animals are the only ones with zero nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sponges, placozoans, and mesozoans are unique among multicellular animals in having no nervous system at all. Their body plans are so simple that chemical diffusion across cells handles what nerves do for every other animal.
wowFactor: Sponges have no brain, no nerves — literally zero neurons — yet they're animals that have survived for 600 million years!

statement: After an Alzheimer's diagnosis, the average life expectancy is three to twelve years — but initial symptoms are often mistaken for normal aging.
quizQuestion: What is the average life expectancy range after an Alzheimer's diagnosis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while the speed of progression varies, the average life expectancy following an Alzheimer's diagnosis is three to twelve years. Critically, initial symptoms are frequently dismissed as normal aging, which delays diagnosis and intervention.
wowFactor: Alzheimer's can be silently progressing for years while doctors and families assume it's just normal aging — by the time it's diagnosed, significant damage is already done.

statement: Up to 10% of Alzheimer's cases are early-onset, affecting people in their 30s to mid-60s — not just the elderly.
quizQuestion: What share of Alzheimer's cases strike people before age 65?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while Alzheimer's most commonly affects people over 65, up to 10% of cases are early-onset, impacting people in their 30s to mid-60s. Women are also diagnosed more often than men, a gap not fully explained by lifespan differences alone.
wowFactor: Alzheimer's can hit people in their 30s — it's not just an old person's disease, and up to 1 in 10 cases are diagnosed before retirement age!

statement: In 2021, stroke was the third biggest cause of death worldwide, responsible for approximately 10% of all deaths.
quizQuestion: In 2021, what rank did stroke hold among the world's leading causes of death?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2021 stroke was the third biggest cause of death globally, accounting for approximately 10% of total deaths. In 2015 it had been ranked second after coronary artery disease, so its relative position has shifted as other diseases changed in prevalence.
wowFactor: Stroke kills 1 in 10 people who die worldwide — it's the third biggest killer on the planet, yet many people don't know the warning signs!

statement: Between 1990 and 2010, stroke incidence fell by ~10% in developed nations but rose by ~10% in developing countries simultaneously.
quizQuestion: Between 1990 and 2010, what happened to stroke rates in the developing world?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, between 1990 and 2010 the annual incidence of stroke decreased by approximately 10% in the developed world but simultaneously increased by about 10% in the developing world — a split driven by diverging access to blood pressure treatment, smoking rates, and diet changes.
wowFactor: While rich countries cut stroke rates, poor countries saw them rise just as fast — the same decade, two opposite trends playing out on the same disease!

statement: There are two main types of stroke: ischemic (caused by blocked blood flow) and hemorrhagic (caused by bleeding into the brain).
quizQuestion: Which process results from a blocked blood vessel rather than bleeding?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ischemic strokes are caused by a blockage stopping blood flow to part of the brain, while hemorrhagic strokes result from bleeding directly into the brain or surrounding membranes. The distinction matters medically — clot-busting drugs that treat ischemic strokes would be catastrophic in hemorrhagic strokes.
wowFactor: The treatment for one type of stroke would kill you if used on the other — diagnosing the right type within minutes is a genuine life-or-death call!

wowFactor: Major depression affects more people than the entire population of Brazil — it's one of the most widespread conditions on the planet, yet it remains deeply stigmatized!

statement: Certain savant abilities — extraordinary skills in specific areas — are more common among autistic people than in any other group.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, certain savant abilities — extraordinary skills in narrow areas like music, memory, or mathematics — are more common among autistic people than in any other group. While the majority of autistic people are not savants, the overlap is striking and genuine.

statement: Unlike most medical specialties, psychiatry has no objective laboratory tests (biomarkers) to confirm diagnoses.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, when the DSM-5 was published in May 2013, NIMH director Thomas Insel immediately noted that psychiatric diagnoses lack validity compared to other medical fields because there are no biomarkers — no blood test, no scan — that can confirm a mental disorder the way a glucose test confirms diabetes.
wowFactor: Psychiatry is the only major medical specialty where every single diagnosis is made without a single lab test — it's still entirely based on observed behavior and self-report.

explanation: According to Wikidata, forensic psychiatry is one of several recognized sub-specialties of psychiatry. It applies psychiatric knowledge within legal proceedings, such as evaluating a defendant's mental fitness to stand trial or assessing criminal responsibility.
wowFactor: There's an entire branch of psychiatry dedicated to the courtroom — forensic psychiatrists regularly testify about whether someone was sane enough to be held responsible for a crime.

statement: Psychiatry has been criticized since its inception for its historical role in social control and the use of coercive treatments.
quizQuestion: Since its inception, psychiatry has faced ongoing criticism for what unusual role?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, psychiatry has faced criticism both from within and outside the profession for its medicalization of mental distress, pharmaceutical industry influence, use of coercion, and its historical role in social control — including the diagnosis of political dissidents as mentally ill in some regimes.
wowFactor: Some governments have weaponized psychiatry to imprison political dissidents under 'mental illness' diagnoses — one of medicine's darkest controversies.

statement: Some researchers have suggested that plants may exhibit forms of intelligence, though this remains controversial.
quizQuestion: Beyond humans and animals, which surprising organisms have researchers proposed may show intelligence?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while intelligence has been well-studied in humans and non-human animals, some researchers have proposed that plants also exhibit forms of intelligence — for example through adaptive root growth and chemical signaling. This remains scientifically controversial.
wowFactor: Scientists are seriously debating whether plants are intelligent — some trees respond to damage by chemically warning their neighbors, which sounds a lot like communication.

statement: The word 'intelligence' rose to scientific prominence as a defined concept during the early 1900s.
quizQuestion: When did the term 'intelligence' rise to scientific prominence as a formal concept?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while philosophers discussed the concept for centuries, the term 'intelligence' as a formal scientific and psychological construct rose to prominence during the early 1900s — the same era that produced the first IQ tests.
wowFactor: Humanity has been 'smart' for hundreds of thousands of years, but we only invented the scientific concept of 'intelligence' about 120 years ago.

statement: The ability to generate electric signals in neurons first appeared in evolution approximately 700 to 800 million years ago, during the Tonian period.
quizQuestion: How many million years ago did neurons first evolve the ability to generate electric signals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, molecular evidence suggests that electrical signaling in neurons first appeared during the Tonian period, roughly 700–800 million years ago. Before neurons evolved, the precursors were peptidergic secretory cells that eventually gained ion channels and post-synaptic scaffolds.
wowFactor: Every thought you've ever had uses technology that first evolved 700 million years ago — before fish, before insects, before most complex life on Earth.

statement: Neurons are found in virtually all animals — the only exceptions are sponges and placozoans.
quizQuestion: Which two animal groups are the only ones that completely lack neurons?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurons are the main components of nervous tissue in all animals except sponges and placozoans. Sponges have no nervous system at all; placozoans are among the simplest animals known, with only a few cell types and no specialized neurons.
wowFactor: Every animal on Earth has neurons — except sponges and one obscure creature called a placozoan. That's how fundamental brains are to animal life.

statement: In 2019, meningitis caused approximately 236,000 deaths worldwide, down from 433,000 deaths in 1990.
quizQuestion: Roughly how many people died from meningitis worldwide in 2019?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2019, meningitis was diagnosed in about 7.7 million people worldwide and killed approximately 236,000 — a significant drop from the 433,000 deaths recorded in 1990, largely due to improved vaccines and treatments.
wowFactor: Deaths from meningitis have almost halved in 30 years — from 433,000 in 1990 to 236,000 in 2019 — thanks to vaccines that simply didn't exist before.

variants.question: True or false: meningitis deaths worldwide decreased significantly between 1990 and 2019.

statement: Dementia is currently the seventh leading cause of death worldwide and generates 10 million new cases every year.
quizQuestion: What is dementia's current global rank as a reason of death?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, dementia is the seventh leading cause of death worldwide with 10 million new cases reported every year — approximately one new case every three seconds. In the UK it has reached the top position as the leading cause of death.
wowFactor: Someone develops dementia every three seconds somewhere on Earth — that's faster than you can read this sentence.

statement: The receptive field of a single rod or cone cell in the eye is the tiny area of light it can individually detect.
quizQuestion: What term describes the specific area of light a single rod or cone can detect?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a receptive field is the specific area of the body or environment to which a receptor cell responds. For a rod or cone in the eye, it is the exact patch of light falling on that cell — the tiniest unit of visual data in the entire visual system.
wowFactor: Every single rod and cone in your eye has its own tiny 'territory' of light it monitors — your vision is built from millions of microscopic lookout posts each watching their own patch of the world.

statement: Neurology is a nonsurgical specialty — its corresponding surgical partner is neurosurgery.
quizQuestion: What is the surgical counterpart to the nonsurgical specialty of neurology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurology is explicitly defined as a nonsurgical specialty — neurologists diagnose and treat nervous system disorders medically, while neurosurgeons perform operations. The two fields collaborate closely but remain distinct.
wowFactor: Neurologists treat your brain with medicine and diagnostics — if you actually need to open the skull, that's a different doctor entirely: a neurosurgeon.

variants.question: True or false: neurology is classified as a nonsurgical medical specialty.

statement: Neurologists treat a broad range of conditions including stroke, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, migraine, and Alzheimer's disease.
quizQuestion: Which medical condition is treated by neurologists, not psychiatrists?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurologists diagnose and treat neurological conditions including Parkinson's disease, stroke, epilepsy, multiple sclerosis, migraine, brain infections, brain tumors, and Alzheimer's disease. Conditions like schizophrenia and bipolar disorder fall under psychiatry.
wowFactor: Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, epilepsy, migraines, and multiple sclerosis — all treated by one specialist: the neurologist, who handles almost everything that can go wrong in your nervous system short of needing a scalpel.

statement: An estimated 15–20% of people with bipolar disorder die by suicide.
quizQuestion: What percentage of people with bipolar disorder are estimated to die by suicide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an estimated 15–20% of people with bipolar disorder die by suicide, and approximately 30–60% attempt suicide during their lifetime — making it one of the most severe mood disorders in terms of mortality risk.
wowFactor: Bipolar disorder carries one of the highest suicide mortality rates of any psychiatric condition — up to 1 in 5 affected individuals, making early treatment literally life-saving.

statement: Bipolar disorder was previously known as manic depression.
quizQuestion: What was bipolar disorder called before its name changed?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bipolar disorder was previously known as manic depression — a name that directly described its two defining poles of mood: mania (abnormally elevated energy) and depression. The modern name 'bipolar' emphasizes the two opposing mood extremes without the stigma of 'madness' implied in older terminology.
wowFactor: For most of the 20th century it was called 'manic depression' — a term still widely used colloquially even though the clinical name changed to 'bipolar disorder' to reduce stigma.

statement: The central nervous system includes the brain, spinal cord, and — surprisingly — the retina of the eye.
quizQuestion: Which unexpected structure is part of the central nervous system alongside the brain and spinal cord?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the central nervous system consists primarily of the brain, spinal cord, and retina. The retina is considered CNS tissue because it develops from brain tissue during embryogenesis — it is literally an outgrowth of the brain that migrated into the eye.
wowFactor: Your retinas are technically part of your brain — they develop from the same embryonic tissue and are considered central nervous system tissue, making the back of your eyes a literal extension of your brain.

variants.question: True or false: the retina is classified as part of the central nervous system, not just a sensory organ.

statement: Only arthropods, cephalopods, and vertebrates have a true brain — sponges and most simpler animals do not.
quizQuestion: Which group of invertebrates, alongside vertebrates, is among the few with a true brain?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only arthropods, cephalopods (like octopuses and squids), and vertebrates have a true brain, though simpler precursor structures exist in some other animals. Sponges and diploblasts lack a central nervous system entirely.
wowFactor: Octopuses, squids, and crabs are among the only invertebrates with a true brain — placing them in exclusive neurological company with all of vertebrate life, while most other animals make do with nerve nets or ganglia.

statement: The central nervous system is protected by the meninges — three layered membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord.
quizQuestion: What protective membranes surround both the brain and spinal cord?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the central nervous system includes the meninges as a key component part alongside the brain and spinal cord. The meninges are three protective membranes (dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater) that encase and shield the brain and spinal cord from physical damage and infection.
wowFactor: Your brain and spinal cord are wrapped in three separate membrane layers called meninges — when these membranes become infected, the result is meningitis, one of medicine's most feared emergencies.

variants.question: True or false: the meninges are membranes that protect the brain and spinal cord.

statement: During a stressful situation, the human body produces two key hormones: adrenaline and cortisol.
quizQuestion: Which two hormones does the human body release during stressful situations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the autonomic nervous system and the HPA axis are the two major systems that respond to stress in humans. Adrenaline is released rapidly via the fight-or-flight pathway, while cortisol — released more slowly via the HPA axis — influences metabolism, immunity, and psychological functions for longer-term adaptation.
wowFactor: Your body runs on two completely different stress chemicals — adrenaline fires in a split second for immediate danger, while cortisol lingers for hours managing the aftermath.

variants.question: Adrenaline and cortisol are stress hormones produced by which organism?

statement: Chronic or severe stress can alter memory, immune function, metabolism, and increase susceptibility to mental illness.
quizQuestion: What does chronic stress do to immune function and memory?
distractors: Has no proven effect on either
explanation: According to Wikipedia, through the SAM and HPA axis mechanisms, stress can alter memory functions, reward pathways, immune function, metabolism, and susceptibility to diseases. Chronic or severe stress is a common risk factor for several mental illnesses.
wowFactor: Prolonged stress doesn't just make you feel bad — it literally rewires memory, hijacks your immune system, and raises your risk of mental illness.

variants.question: True or false: chronic stress is a common risk factor for several mental illnesses.

statement: ADHD is primarily genetic, with a heritability rate of 70–80% according to meta-analyses.
quizQuestion: What is the heritability rate of ADHD according to meta-analyses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, meta-analyses have shown that ADHD is primarily genetic with a heritability rate of 70–80%, where risk factors are highly accumulative. Environmental risks are not related to social or familial factors and exert their effects very early in life, during the prenatal or early postnatal period.
wowFactor: ADHD is more heritable than many people assume — your genes account for up to 80% of the risk, which is on par with the heritability of height.

variants.question: True or false: ADHD is primarily caused by social and family environment rather than genetics.

statement: People with ADHD can experience hyperfocus — a paradoxical ability to sustain extreme concentration on certain tasks despite generally struggling with attention.
quizQuestion: What is the ADHD symptom of extreme sustained concentration on one task called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while ADHD involves a lack of sustained attention to tasks, inhibitory deficits can also lead to difficulty interrupting an already ongoing response pattern — manifesting as hyperfocus, the perseveration of actions despite a change in context. This is colloquially called hyperfocus and is associated with risks such as addiction and certain types of offending behaviour.
wowFactor: ADHD isn't just an attention deficit — people with ADHD can also lock onto a single task so intensely they literally can't stop, which itself carries its own risks.

statement: In mormyrid fish, the cerebellum can be as large as — or even larger than — the entire cerebrum.
quizQuestion: In which animal can the cerebellum grow as large as or larger than the entire cerebrum?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, although the cerebellum is usually smaller than the cerebrum, in some animals such as mormyrid fishes it may be as large as the cerebrum or even larger. In humans, the cerebellum plays an important role in motor control, attention, language, and emotional regulation.
wowFactor: There are fish whose 'little brain' outgrows the entire rest of their brain — mormyrid fish have a proportionally massive cerebellum unlike almost anything else in nature.

statement: The cerebellar cortex is a thin, continuous sheet of tissue folded like an accordion, hiding far more surface area than its exterior grooves suggest.
quizQuestion: What everyday object does the folding pattern of the cerebellar cortex resemble?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the parallel grooves on the cerebellum's surface conceal the fact that the cerebellar cortex is actually a thin, continuous layer of tissue tightly folded in the style of an accordion. This gives the cerebellum a massive signal-processing capability despite its compact size.
wowFactor: The cerebellum hides an astonishing amount of processing power inside accordion-like folds — its surface looks simple from the outside but conceals a huge hidden sheet of neural tissue.

statement: The word 'coma' comes from the Ancient Greek word 'koma', meaning 'deep sleep'.
quizQuestion: What does the Ancient Greek word 'koma' — origin of 'coma' — literally mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the word coma derives from the Ancient Greek 'koma' meaning 'deep sleep'. A coma is a prolonged state of deep unconsciousness where a person cannot be awakened, fails to respond to stimuli, and lacks a normal sleep-wake cycle.
wowFactor: Ancient Greeks had a word for the deepest possible sleep — and it became the medical term we use today for one of medicine's most dramatic states of unconsciousness.

variants.question: True or false: the word 'coma' originates from Ancient Greek meaning 'deep sleep'.

statement: Consciousness depends on two brain systems working together: the cerebral cortex and the reticular activating system (RAS) in the brainstem.
quizQuestion: Which brainstem structure, alongside the cortex, must function to maintain consciousness?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, consciousness is maintained by the activation of the cerebral cortex — the gray matter forming the brain's outermost layer — and by the reticular activating system (RAS), a structure in the brainstem. Coma results when either or both of these systems are disrupted.
wowFactor: Your consciousness is kept on by a tiny relay station in your brainstem called the RAS — damage it even slightly and the entire cortex goes dark, no matter how healthy the rest of the brain is.

statement: Neuroscience draws on more than a dozen disciplines including physiology, computer science, physics, chemistry, and mathematical modeling to study the nervous system.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, neuroscience is a multidisciplinary science that combines physiology, anatomy, molecular biology, developmental biology, cytology, psychology, physics, computer science, chemistry, medicine, statistics, and mathematical modeling. This breadth reflects the complexity of studying neurons, glia, and neural circuits from molecular to cognitive scales.
wowFactor: Neuroscience officially lists computer science as one of its core fields — the study of the brain and the study of computing have always been deeply intertwined.

statement: According to the WHO, mental health is not merely the absence of mental illness — it is a state of well-being in which a person realizes their abilities and can contribute to their community.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, the WHO defines mental health as a 'state of well-being in which the individual realizes their abilities, can cope with the normal stresses of life, can work productively and fruitfully, and can contribute to their community' — explicitly framing it as more than just the absence of mental illness.
wowFactor: The WHO says true mental health isn't just 'not being sick' — it's actively thriving and contributing to your community.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the United States about 9% of people develop PTSD at some point in their lives, and about 3.5% have it in any given year. Crucially, most trauma survivors do not develop the disorder — highlighting that trauma exposure and PTSD are not the same thing.

statement: PTSD is more common in women than in men, despite being historically associated with male combat veterans.

quizQuestion: Which 17th-century diarist described symptoms now recognized as PTSD after a famous disaster?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Samuel Pepys' diary contains one of the earliest documented descriptions of post-traumatic symptoms, written after the 1666 Fire of London. PTSD-like symptoms have been described since ancient Greece, but Pepys gives us a rare named, datable personal account.
wowFactor: A 17th-century diarist accidentally documented PTSD 300 years before it had a name — his nightmares after the Great Fire of London read like a modern case file.

statement: Young children with PTSD are less likely to show classic distress — instead, they may re-enact traumatic memories through play.
quizQuestion: How do young children often express PTSD differently from adults?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, young children are less likely to show classic PTSD distress symptoms; instead they may express traumatic memories through play — a unique developmental presentation that can make PTSD harder to recognize in children.
wowFactor: A child who keeps replaying a 'scary scene' in their toys might not be playing — they could be processing trauma the only way their developing brain knows how.

variants.question: True or false: Young children are less likely than adults to show direct distress symptoms of PTSD.
