Young children may express traumatic memories through ___ rather than showing visible distress.
Which age group is most likely to express PTSD through play re-enactment rather than direct distress?

sensitivityNote: Discusses childhood trauma; framed clinically and without graphic content.
visualDescription: A pixel-art child crouches on a bedroom floor, tiny toy cars arranged in a crash pattern again and again, eyes distant, the room otherwise quiet and dark.

statement: The term 'neurosis' has been officially removed from both the WHO's ICD and the APA's DSM — the two most authoritative psychiatric diagnostic systems in the world.
quizQuestion: Which two major diagnostic systems no longer use the term 'neurosis'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'neurosis' is no longer used in diagnostic categories by either the WHO's International Classification of Diseases (ICD) or the APA's Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM). The American Heritage Medical Dictionary of 2007 states it is 'no longer used in psychiatric diagnosis.'
wowFactor: Neurosis was once a cornerstone of psychiatry — now it's officially been erased from both of the world's major diagnostic bibles.

variants.question: True or false: The term 'neurosis' is still used in the current DSM for diagnosing mental disorders.
According to the 2007 American Heritage Medical Dictionary, neurosis is 'no longer used in psychiatric ___'.
Which once-central psychiatric term was officially removed from both the ICD and DSM?
A psychiatrist in 1970 diagnosed 'anxiety neurosis.' What modern ICD-11 concept has replaced this framing?

sensitivityNote: Discusses psychiatric terminology history; no clinical triggers.
visualDescription: A pixel-art medical textbook open to a page with the word NEUROSIS crossed out in red ink, two official stamps — ICD and DSM — pressed over it in bureaucratic purple.

statement: Neurosis was historically distinguished from psychosis by a key difference: neurosis preserved a person's grip on reality, while psychosis involved losing it.
quizQuestion: What key feature distinguishes neurosis from psychosis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neurosis is distinguished from psychosis in that psychosis refers to a loss of touch with reality — while neurosis, even in its most distressing form, does not involve this break. This distinction was foundational in 20th-century psychiatry.
wowFactor: The original psychiatric dividing line between neurosis and psychosis was simple: one keeps you in touch with reality, the other breaks that connection entirely.

variants.question: True or false: Neurosis, unlike psychosis, does not involve a loss of touch with reality.
Psychosis refers to a loss of touch with ___, which distinguishes it from neurosis.
Which condition is defined by a loss of touch with reality, in contrast to neurosis?
A patient has intense anxiety but knows their fears are excessive and remains oriented. Which historical category does this match?

sensitivityNote: Psychiatric concepts discussed clinically.
visualDescription: Two pixel-art figures stand on either side of a cracked glass wall — one sees the room clearly through the cracks, the other sees a fragmented, distorted reflection.

statement: Though 'neurosis' was dropped from psychiatric diagnoses, its descendant 'neuroticism' survives as one of the Big Five personality traits — a tendency toward anxiety and emotional instability.
quizQuestion: Which of the Big Five personality traits descends from the concept of neurosis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while 'neurosis' was dropped from both the DSM and ICD, its descendant term 'neuroticism' lives on as one of the Big Five personality traits — a tendency toward anxiousness and mental collapse under stress. The ICD-11 includes a related concept called 'negative affectivity.'
wowFactor: Neurosis was officially banned from psychiatry, but it snuck back in through personality science — now it's one of the five core traits that define every human personality.

variants.question: True or false: Neuroticism is one of the Big Five personality traits and descends from the concept of neurosis.
The ICD-11 includes the concept of 'negative ___' as a modern replacement for neurosis-related conditions.
Which outdated psychiatric term is the conceptual ancestor of the Big Five trait 'neuroticism'?
A personality psychologist measures a patient's tendency toward anxiety and collapse. Which Big Five trait are they assessing?

sensitivityNote: Personality science; no clinical triggers.
visualDescription: A pixel-art five-sided star chart glows on a dark screen, one tip labeled NEUROTICISM pulsing orange — descended from a ghostly word fading behind it: NEUROSIS.

statement: In 2015, encephalitis was estimated to have affected 4.3 million people worldwide and caused 150,000 deaths — making brain inflammation a significant global killer.
quizQuestion: How many deaths did encephalitis cause worldwide in 2015?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis — inflammation of the brain — affected an estimated 4.3 million people and caused 150,000 deaths globally in 2015. Causes range from viral infections like herpes simplex and rabies to autoimmune diseases.
wowFactor: Brain inflammation kills 150,000 people a year — roughly equal to the entire population of a mid-sized city — yet most people can't name a single encephalitis case.

variants.question: How many people were estimated to be affected by encephalitis worldwide in 2015?
True or false: In 2015, encephalitis caused more than 100,000 deaths worldwide.
Which brain condition affected 4.3 million people globally and killed 150,000 in 2015?
A public health report cites a brain disease with 4.3 million cases and 150,000 deaths in 2015. What is it?

sensitivityNote: Disease statistics; no graphic medical content.
visualDescription: A pixel-art globe with 150,000 tiny red dots scattered across every continent, each dot a life lost to brain inflammation, the scale dwarfing a small city skyline beneath.

statement: Encephalitis can be caused by viruses like herpes simplex and rabies, by bacteria, fungi, parasites, autoimmune diseases, or certain medications — and in many cases the cause is never found.
quizQuestion: Which well-known virus is listed as a cause of encephalitis alongside rabies?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis — inflammation of the brain — can be caused by herpes simplex virus, rabies virus, bacteria, fungi, parasites, autoimmune diseases, or medications. In many cases, no cause is identified, making it a diagnostic challenge.
wowFactor: The same herpes virus responsible for cold sores is also one of the most common causes of life-threatening brain inflammation.

variants.question: True or false: In many encephalitis cases, the cause remains unknown even after full investigation.
Encephalitis can be caused not only by viruses but also by autoimmune diseases and certain ___.
A patient with encephalitis tests negative for all known pathogens. What does this reveal about the disease?
distractors: It always has a viral cause

variants.question: Which type of infection is the herpes simplex virus most unusually capable of causing beyond its typical presentation?

sensitivityNote: Medical causes; clinical framing, no graphic content.
visualDescription: A pixel-art cross-section of a brain with tiny invader icons — a virus, a bacterium, a fungal spore, a question mark — each glowing red as they infiltrate the neural tissue.

statement: Encephalitis symptoms span a disturbing range — from headache, fever, and confusion to aphasia, seizures, hallucinations, and memory problems.
quizQuestion: Which speech-related symptom can encephalitis cause alongside seizures and hallucinations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis can cause aphasia (loss of ability to speak or understand language) as a complication — alongside seizures, hallucinations, memory problems, and hearing difficulties. This range reflects how widespread brain inflammation disrupts multiple neural systems.
wowFactor: Encephalitis doesn't just cause a headache — it can take away your ability to speak, make you hallucinate, and scramble your memory, all at once.

variants.question: True or false: Encephalitis can cause both hallucinations and memory problems as complications.
Encephalitis complications may include seizures, hallucinations, trouble speaking, and problems with ___.
Which brain inflammation condition can cause aphasia, seizures, and hallucinations simultaneously?
A patient develops fever, confusion, then suddenly loses the ability to speak. Which condition fits this symptom progression?

visualDescription: A pixel-art human head in cross-section glows angry red, while speech bubbles dissolve into static, a seizure lightning bolt cracks the air, and ghostly shapes float near the ear.

statement: The cerebrum contains the hippocampus, basal ganglia, and olfactory bulb as subcortical structures beneath the cerebral cortex.
quizQuestion: Which famous memory structure is housed inside the cerebrum?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cerebrum contains several subcortical structures including the hippocampus (critical for forming memories), the basal ganglia (movement coordination), and the olfactory bulb (smell processing) — all tucked beneath the outer cortex.
wowFactor: The hippocampus — the brain's memory recorder — lives inside the cerebrum, meaning the part of you that stores every experience is nested inside your largest brain region.

variants.question: True or false: the hippocampus is a subcortical structure within the cerebrum.
The cerebrum contains the hippocampus, basal ganglia, and ___ as subcortical structures.

visualDescription: A zoomed pixel art cutaway of the cerebrum reveals a seahorse-shaped hippocampus glowing gold deep inside the brain tissue, surrounded by layered grey cortex.

statement: Secondary brain tumors — cancers that spread from elsewhere in the body — are about four times more common than primary brain tumors.
quizQuestion: How much more common are secondary brain tumors than primary ones?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, secondary (metastatic) brain tumors — which spread from cancers elsewhere — are roughly four times as common as tumors that originate in the brain itself, with about half of these metastases coming from lung cancer.
wowFactor: Most brain tumors didn't start in the brain at all — they're invaders from other cancers, and they outnumber native brain cancers four to one.

variants.question: True or false: secondary brain tumors are more common than primary brain tumors.
What is the most common cancer source for brain metastases?
Which type of brain tumor is four times more common — primary or secondary?
A patient has a brain tumor that originated from lung cancer. What type is this?

sensitivityNote: Cancer topic — appropriate for adult audiences
visualDescription: Pixel art of a human brain cross-section with four glowing orange invasion markers versus one small red native tumor marker, illustrating the 4-to-1 ratio.

statement: The average five-year survival rate for all malignant brain cancers in the United States is 33%.
quizQuestion: What is the average 5-year survival rate for malignant brain cancers in the US?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, roughly one in three people diagnosed with a malignant brain tumor in the United States survives five years — though this average masks wide variation: malignant glioblastomas have very poor outcomes, while benign meningiomas typically do well.
wowFactor: Only 1 in 3 people with a malignant brain tumor survives five years — but the real story is that benign meningiomas can be cured while glioblastomas remain devastatingly deadly.

variants.question: True or false: malignant glioblastomas have very poor survival outcomes.
Which brain tumor type generally has the best survival outcomes?
A doctor says a patient's brain tumor has the worst typical prognosis. Which type is it?
Benign meningiomas usually have ___ outcomes, while malignant glioblastomas usually have very poor outcomes.

sensitivityNote: Cancer survival statistics — adult audience appropriate
visualDescription: A pixel art bar chart shows one in three glowing human silhouettes surviving, two darkened — a stark visual representation of brain cancer survival odds.

statement: Intellectual disability is diagnosed when IQ falls below 70 and the person shows deficits in at least two adaptive behaviors affecting daily life.
quizQuestion: Below what IQ score is intellectual disability typically diagnosed?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the clinical threshold for intellectual disability is an IQ below 70 combined with deficits in at least two adaptive behaviors — practical skills like communication, self-care, and social functioning — that first appear during childhood.
wowFactor: Intellectual disability isn't diagnosed on IQ alone — you also need real-world skill deficits in two life areas, meaning the same IQ score could qualify one person but not another.

variants.question: True or false: an IQ below 70 alone is sufficient to diagnose intellectual disability.
Besides a low IQ, what else is required to diagnose intellectual disability?
Intellectual disability requires an IQ below ___ and deficits in at least two adaptive behaviors.
A child scores 65 on an IQ test but has no problems with daily living skills. Do they meet criteria for intellectual disability?

sensitivityNote: Clinical diagnostic criteria — neutral and educational
visualDescription: Pixel art of a clipboard with two checkboxes — one showing a number below 70 in a brain icon, another showing two crossed-out daily activity icons — depicting the dual diagnosis criteria.

statement: Down syndrome and fragile X syndrome are the two most recognized examples of syndromic intellectual disability.
quizQuestion: Which two syndromes are the classic examples of syndromic intellectual disability?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, syndromic intellectual disability pairs cognitive deficits with other distinctive medical or behavioral features. Down syndrome (trisomy 21) and fragile X syndrome are the two most frequently cited examples — together accounting for a large share of identified genetic causes.
wowFactor: Fragile X syndrome is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability, yet most people have never heard of it — Down syndrome gets the headlines while fragile X quietly affects 1 in 4,000 males.

variants.question: True or false: Down syndrome is an example of syndromic intellectual disability.
Fragile X syndrome is a classic example of which broader condition?
Down syndrome and fragile X syndrome are examples of ___ intellectual disability.
A geneticist diagnoses a child with intellectual disability plus distinctive physical features. This best matches which subtype?

sensitivityNote: Genetic conditions topic — clinical and educational
visualDescription: Pixel art of two chromosomal diagrams side by side — one with a visible extra chromosome 21 and one with a fragile break on the X chromosome — set on a lab bench.

statement: Unlike the brain and spinal cord, the peripheral nervous system has no skull or vertebral column protecting it, and no blood-brain barrier — leaving it exposed to toxins.
quizQuestion: What key protection does the peripheral nervous system lack that the brain has?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the peripheral nervous system lies outside the skull and spine and lacks the blood-brain barrier — a selective filter that keeps harmful substances out of the central nervous system. This is why peripheral nerves are far more vulnerable to toxins like heavy metals, alcohol, and certain chemotherapy drugs.
wowFactor: The nerves running through your arms and legs have zero chemical protection — every toxin you absorb can reach them directly, which is why alcoholics and chemotherapy patients often develop peripheral nerve damage.

variants.question: True or false: the peripheral nervous system is protected by the blood-brain barrier.
Which nervous system division is exposed to toxins because it lacks the blood-brain barrier?
Unlike the CNS, the peripheral nervous system is not protected by the vertebral column or the ___.

visualDescription: Pixel art split image: left side shows the brain inside a glowing protective skull barrier; right side shows exposed nerve branches running through an arm with no barrier, vulnerable glowing particles drifting toward them.

statement: The peripheral nervous system is divided into a somatic division (voluntary) and an autonomic division (involuntary), each with sensory and motor sectors.
quizQuestion: What are the two main divisions of the peripheral nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the peripheral nervous system splits into the somatic division — which governs voluntary muscle control and conscious sensation — and the autonomic division, which controls involuntary functions like heart rate, digestion, and gland secretion.
wowFactor: Your peripheral nervous system runs two parallel networks simultaneously: one for every move you choose to make, and another silently running your heart, lungs, and gut without any conscious input.

variants.question: True or false: the autonomic nervous system controls involuntary actions like heart rate.
Which division of the peripheral nervous system controls voluntary movements?
The peripheral nervous system divides into a somatic division and an ___ division.

visualDescription: Pixel art diagram of a human silhouette with two color-coded nerve networks overlaid — blue voluntary pathways running to arms and legs, orange involuntary pathways threading to the heart and gut.

statement: The peripheral nervous system's main function is to connect the central nervous system to the limbs and organs, acting as a relay between the brain and the rest of the body.
quizQuestion: What is the primary function of the peripheral nervous system?
distractors: Generate electrical impulses in the brain
acceptableAnswers: connect brain to limbs and organs
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the peripheral nervous system acts as the communication relay connecting the brain and spinal cord to all limbs and organs — carrying sensory signals inward and motor commands outward. Without it, the brain would be completely isolated from the body.
wowFactor: The peripheral nervous system is literally your body's wiring harness — without it, your brain couldn't feel your fingertips or move a single muscle outside the skull.

variants.question: True or false: the peripheral nervous system connects the brain and spinal cord to the limbs and organs.
Which nervous system division acts as a relay between the CNS and the rest of the body?
The peripheral nervous system connects the ___ to the limbs and organs.
Damage to which system would sever communication between the spinal cord and the arms?

visualDescription: Pixel art of a brain and spinal cord glowing at center, with bright nerve cables radiating outward like wires to hands, feet, eyes, and heart against a dark anatomical background.

statement: The human brain weighs approximately 1.2 kilograms — about 2% of body weight yet consuming roughly 20% of the body's energy.
quizQuestion: How much does an average human brain weigh?
explanation: Wikidata records the human brain's mass as 1.2 kilograms. Despite being only about 2% of body weight, the brain is metabolically voracious — it demands a disproportionate share of the body's oxygen and glucose at all times.
wowFactor: Your brain is only 2% of your body weight but devours a fifth of all the energy your body produces — the most expensive organ you own!

variants.question: True or false: the human brain weighs more than 3 kilograms.
Which human organ has a mass of approximately 1.2 kilograms?

The human brain weighs approximately ___ kilograms.

visualDescription: A pixel-art balance scale with a glowing brain on one side and a bag labeled '1.2 kg' on the other, perfectly balanced in a dimly lit laboratory.

statement: The two hemispheres of the human brain are connected by a massive bundle of nerve fibres called the corpus callosum — the brain's largest internal highway.
quizQuestion: What is the name of the largest nerve tract connecting the brain's two hemispheres?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the corpus callosum is the largest commissural nerve tract connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres. Without it, the two halves of the brain cannot share information — a fact famously revealed by 'split-brain' surgery patients who showed dramatically different responses from each hand.
wowFactor: Cut the corpus callosum and your brain literally becomes two separate minds — split-brain patients' left and right hands sometimes fight each other over simple tasks!

variants.question: True or false: the corpus callosum is the largest nerve tract connecting the brain's left and right hemispheres.
Which brain structure is the main communication bridge between the left and right cerebral hemispheres?
A surgeon severs a patient's largest inter-hemispheric nerve tract. Which structure did she cut?
The brain's two hemispheres are joined by a large nerve tract called the ___.

visualDescription: A cross-section pixel-art brain with a glowing white bridge arching between two distinct blue and orange hemispheres, sparks of data crossing the bridge like tiny trains.

statement: The brain is isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier, a selective shield that blocks most pathogens and chemicals from entering brain tissue.
quizQuestion: What protective barrier isolates the brain from the general bloodstream?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brain is suspended in cerebrospinal fluid and isolated from the bloodstream by the blood-brain barrier. This biological filter is so selective that it also makes delivering drugs to the brain extremely difficult — a major challenge in treating brain diseases.
wowFactor: The blood-brain barrier is so strict that most medicines simply can't reach the brain — it's why treating brain diseases like Alzheimer's is so brutally hard!

variants.question: True or false: the blood-brain barrier makes it easier to deliver drugs directly to brain tissue.
Which organ in the body is protected by a selective barrier that blocks most blood-borne substances?

The brain is isolated from the bloodstream by the ___.

visualDescription: A pixel-art cross-section of a blood vessel next to brain tissue, with tiny red blood cells bouncing off a glowing force-field wall while small green molecules slip through selected gates.

statement: Nervous tissue is composed of neurons and glial cells; bundles of neuron axons form the nerves of the peripheral nervous system and the tracts of the central nervous system.
quizQuestion: What are bundles of axons in the peripheral nervous system called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the peripheral nervous system (PNS) bundles of axons are called nerves, while in the central nervous system (CNS) the equivalent structures are called tracts. The naming distinction matters clinically — PNS nerves can regenerate after injury; CNS tracts generally cannot.
wowFactor: Cut a nerve in your arm and it can grow back — cut the equivalent tract in your spinal cord and it usually can't. The same tissue plays by different regeneration rules depending on where it is!

variants.question: True or false: in the central nervous system, bundles of axons are called tracts rather than nerves.
In which nervous system division are bundles of axons called 'nerves' rather than 'tracts'?

visualDescription: A pixel-art diagram showing thick cable-like nerve bundles branching into an arm on one side and thin glowing tracts running up a spinal column on the other, both labeled.

statement: Glial cells (neuroglia) in nervous tissue do not transmit signals themselves but assist nerve impulse propagation and supply nutrients to neurons.
quizQuestion: What role do glial cells play in nervous tissue?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neuroglia (glial cells) assist the propagation of nerve impulses and provide nutrients to neurons — they are the unsung workhorses of the nervous system. Without glial support, neurons would starve and lose the insulation (myelin) needed to conduct signals rapidly.
wowFactor: Glial cells outnumber neurons in the brain and do none of the glamorous signalling — they just quietly keep neurons alive, fed, and insulated. Without them, your thoughts would grind to a halt!

variants.question: True or false: glial cells transmit electrical nerve impulses just like neurons do.

statement: The nervous system performs five core functions: sensory input, integration, muscle and gland control, homeostasis, and mental activity.
quizQuestion: Which of these is NOT listed as a core function of the nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the five core functions of the nervous system are sensory input, integration, control of muscles and glands, homeostasis, and mental activity. Hormone secretion is primarily the endocrine system's role — the nervous system can trigger glands but does not itself secrete hormones into the bloodstream.
wowFactor: The nervous system manages everything from your heartbeat to your deepest thought — it's a single tissue type doing five completely different jobs simultaneously!

variants.question: True or false: homeostasis is listed as a core function of the nervous system.
How many core functions does the nervous system perform according to biology?
Which body system is primarily responsible for sensory input, integration, and homeostasis?
The five functions of the nervous system include sensory input, integration, homeostasis, mental activity, and control of muscles and ___.

statement: The brainstem makes up only about 2.6% of the brain's total weight, yet it controls heart rate, breathing rate, and the sleep cycle.
quizQuestion: What percentage of total brain weight does the brainstem make up?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brainstem is very small, comprising only around 2.6% of the brain's total weight, yet it has critical roles: regulating heart rate, breathing rate, and the body's sleep cycle. Damage to this tiny structure is almost always fatal or catastrophically disabling.
wowFactor: The brainstem is just 2.6% of your brain — a sliver the size of your thumb — yet without it you cannot breathe, your heart stops, and you can never sleep again!

variants.question: True or false: the brainstem controls heart rate and breathing despite making up less than 5% of brain weight.

statement: The brainstem is composed of three distinct structures: the midbrain, the pons, and the medulla oblongata.
quizQuestion: Which three structures make up the human brainstem?
distractors: Frontal lobe, temporal lobe, parietal lobe
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the brainstem consists of the midbrain, the pons, and the medulla oblongata — each with distinct responsibilities. The medulla regulates automatic functions like heartbeat; the pons relays signals between the cerebrum and cerebellum; the midbrain processes visual and auditory reflexes.
wowFactor: The brainstem is actually three parts stacked like floors of a building — and each floor runs a different set of automatic life-support functions without you ever thinking about them!

variants.question: True or false: the pons is one of the three structures that make up the brainstem.

statement: The human cerebral cortex contains between 14 and 16 billion neurons, organised into six horizontal layers and radial columns.
quizQuestion: How many neurons are found in the human cerebral cortex?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are between 14 and 16 billion neurons in the human cerebral cortex, organised into horizontal cortical layers and radial cortical columns. The cortex is the seat of consciousness, language, memory, and thought — packed into a structure only a few millimetres thick.
wowFactor: The thin outer layer of your brain — just a few millimetres thick — packs up to 16 billion neurons, and if you unfolded all its wrinkles it would cover a small tablecloth!

variants.question: True or false: the cerebral cortex contains more than 10 billion neurons.

statement: The cerebral cortex is heavily folded — a feature called gyrification — which dramatically increases its surface area within the confined space of the skull.
quizQuestion: Why is the cerebral cortex folded into ridges and grooves?
distractors: To anchor it to the skull
To separate left and right lobes
acceptableAnswers: fit more cortex in the skull
greater surface area in the cranium
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cortical folding (gyrification) provides a greater surface area within the confined volume of the cranium and is crucial for brain circuitry and functional organisation. In humans, the majority of the cerebral cortex is actually hidden inside the sulci (grooves), not visible from outside.
wowFactor: Most of your cerebral cortex is buried inside its own folds — like a crumpled sheet stuffed in a box. Unfolded, it would be roughly the size of a medium pizza!

variants.question: True or false: in mammals with very small brains, the cerebral cortex is smooth rather than folded.

statement: The six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cerebral cortex, with the older allocortex (3–4 layers) making up the remaining 10%.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the cerebral cortex does the six-layered neocortex comprise?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the six-layered neocortex makes up approximately 90% of the cortex, with the allocortex (which has three or four layers) making up the remainder. The neocortex is evolutionarily the newest brain region and is primarily responsible for higher cognitive functions such as language, reasoning, and consciousness.
wowFactor: Nine-tenths of your cerebral cortex is the 'new' neocortex — the evolutionarily recent layer responsible for everything that makes humans distinctively human, from language to abstract thought!

variants.question: True or false: the neocortex is characterised by having six neuronal layers.

visualDescription: A pixel-art cortex cross-section divided 90/10 — a large gold section labeled 'Neocortex (6 layers)' and a small blue section labeled 'Allocortex (3-4 layers)' at the edge.

statement: Ganglia are clusters of neuron cell bodies located outside the brain and spinal cord, in the peripheral nervous system.
quizQuestion: Where in the nervous system are ganglia (neuron clusters) found?
acceptableAnswers: outside the brain and spinal cord
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ganglia are groups of neuron cell bodies specifically in the peripheral nervous system — outside the brain and spinal cord. This includes dorsal root ganglia (relaying sensory signals from the body) and autonomic ganglia that control involuntary functions.
wowFactor: Ganglia are basically the nervous system's relay stations parked outside your brain — tiny hubs of neurons scattered through your body that keep signals moving without routing everything through your head.

variants.question: What is a cluster of neuron cell bodies in the peripheral nervous system called?
True or false: ganglia are found inside the brain and spinal cord.

statement: The autonomic nervous system has both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia, each housing the cell bodies of postganglionic neurons that control involuntary body functions.
quizQuestion: Which nervous system division contains both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ganglia exist in two major arms of the autonomic nervous system: sympathetic ganglia (fight-or-flight) and parasympathetic ganglia (rest-and-digest). Both types contain the cell bodies of postganglionic neurons that carry the final signal to organs and glands.
wowFactor: Your body runs two parallel autopilot networks — fight-or-flight and rest-and-digest — and each one has its own dedicated neuron relay stations (ganglia) dotted along the way.

variants.question: True or false: the autonomic nervous system contains both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia.

statement: A pseudoganglion looks exactly like a ganglion under the microscope but contains almost no neuron cell bodies — only nerve fibers.
quizQuestion: What makes a pseudoganglion different from a real ganglion?
distractors: It is found in the brain
It has no nerve fibers at all
It belongs to the somatic system only
It is made of glial cells only
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a pseudoganglion is a structural mimic — it has the swollen, nodule-like appearance of a true ganglion but lacks significant numbers of neuron cell bodies inside. It contains mainly nerve fibers, making the resemblance superficial.
wowFactor: The nervous system has fake relay stations called pseudoganglia — they look identical to real ganglia from the outside but are essentially hollow decoys with no neuron cell bodies inside.

statement: The somatosensory system handles three distinct jobs: detecting external stimuli (touch), detecting internal stimuli, and regulating body position and balance (proprioception).
quizQuestion: Which sense does the somatosensory system regulate alongside touch and internal stimuli?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory system has three main functions: perceiving external stimuli (like touch and vibration), perceiving internal stimuli, and regulating body position and balance through proprioception. Proprioception is the hidden 'sixth sense' that tells your brain where your limbs are without looking.
wowFactor: You have a secret sixth sense called proprioception — part of your somatosensory system — that tells your brain exactly where every limb is in space, even with your eyes closed.

statement: The somatosensory system has two main subdivisions: one for mechanosensory touch (pressure, vibration, tension) and one for nociception (pain and temperature).
quizQuestion: What are the two main subdivisions of the somatosensory system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory system is thought to have two subdivisions: mechanosensory detection (light touch, vibration, pressure, skin tension) and nociception (pain and harmful temperature signals). Nociceptors are the specialized pain receptors; thermoreceptors handle normal temperature ranges.
wowFactor: Your skin runs two completely separate detection networks in parallel — one mapping gentle touch and pressure, another on constant alert for anything that could hurt you.

statement: The somatosensory cortex contains a map of the entire body called a sensory homunculus, where neighboring neurons represent nearby body locations.
quizQuestion: What is the brain's body-surface map in the somatosensory cortex called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the somatosensory cortex is organized so that neighboring neurons represent nearby body locations — creating a distorted but complete 'map' of the body's surface called a sensory homunculus. Areas with dense touch receptors (like fingertips and lips) take up far more cortical space than areas like the back.
wowFactor: Your brain contains a warped little body-map called a homunculus — your fingertips and lips are grotesquely huge on it because they're packed with touch receptors, while your back is tiny.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, archaeological evidence from Peru shows cocaine use stretching back to approximately 8000 BCE — roughly 10,000 years ago. This makes it one of the earliest documented stimulant substances in human history, long predating modern pharmacology.
wowFactor: Humans were chewing cocaine leaves in Peru 10,000 years ago — archaeological evidence pushes its use back to 8000 BCE, predating the pyramids by over 4,000 years.

statement: Stimulants have been used to promote aggression in combatants during wartime, both historically and in modern conflicts.
quizQuestion: What controversial purpose have stimulants served in wartime beyond treating illness?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, stimulants have a documented history of military use to promote aggression and endurance in combatants — a practice that spans both historical and modern conflicts. This is distinct from their medical use (e.g., treating narcolepsy or ADHD) and represents one of the darker applications of psychostimulants.
wowFactor: Armies have deliberately drugged soldiers with stimulants to make them more aggressive — a practice that still occurs in modern conflicts, not just ancient history.

statement: Caffeine works as a stimulant by blocking the activity of adenosine — the brain's natural sleep-promoting chemical — rather than by directly exciting neurons.
quizQuestion: How does caffeine stimulate the brain — by exciting neurons or by something?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, caffeine works not by directly stimulating neurons but by blocking the activity of endogenous agents that promote sleep — specifically adenosine. When adenosine can't bind to its receptors, the brain stays alert. This is fundamentally different from stimulants like cocaine or amphetamine that directly boost excitatory neurotransmitters.
wowFactor: Caffeine doesn't actually energize you — it just mutes your brain's 'go to sleep' signal (adenosine) so your natural alertness can keep running. It's a blocker, not a booster.

statement: Concussion symptoms typically last up to 2 weeks in adults but can persist for up to 4 weeks in children.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, concussion symptoms are not unusual when they last 2 weeks in adults but can extend up to 4 weeks in children. This means children need longer recovery monitoring after a head injury, and returning to school or sport too soon carries greater risk for them.
wowFactor: A child's brain takes twice as long to recover from a concussion as an adult's — up to 4 weeks versus 2 — making rushing kids back to sport after a head knock genuinely dangerous.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, fewer than 10% of sports-related concussions among children are associated with loss of consciousness. This means the common belief that 'blacking out' is a hallmark of concussion is wrong — most concussions happen without any loss of consciousness at all.
wowFactor: The idea that concussion means blacking out is a dangerous myth — fewer than 1 in 10 sports concussions in children ever cause a loss of consciousness. Most go completely unnoticed.

statement: Prolonged bed rest after a concussion can actually slow recovery and worsen depression and anxiety — recommended rest is only 1 to 2 days.
quizQuestion: Why is prolonged bed rest after a concussion now discouraged by doctors?
correctAnswer: It slows recovery and worsens mood
distractors: It causes muscle atrophy too quickly
It prevents the brain from forming new memories
It increases risk of second concussion
explanation: According to Wikipedia, treatment of concussion includes relative rest for no more than 1-2 days, followed by aerobic exercise and gradual return to activity. Prolonged rest may slow recovery and result in greater depression and anxiety — a counterintuitive finding that reversed decades of 'just rest it off' medical advice.
wowFactor: Doctors used to say 'just rest in a dark room' after a concussion — but science found prolonged bed rest actually makes you heal slower and feel worse. Light exercise after 48 hours is now the prescription.

wowFactor: The most common brain injury isn't a stroke or infection — it's a plain old knock to the head from a fall or accident.

statement: Brain injuries can cause two waves of damage: primary injuries that occur at the moment of trauma, and secondary injuries that develop afterward due to the body's own physiological responses.

statement: People who survive a malaria infection develop only partial immunity — they can be reinfected, but with milder symptoms. This immunity disappears if exposure stops.
quizQuestion: What happens to malaria immunity if a person stops being exposed to the disease?
distractors: It shifts into a dormant form
explanation: According to Wikipedia, malaria survivors develop only partial immunity — they can still be reinfected but experience milder symptoms. Crucially, this partial immunity disappears over months to years without continued exposure. This explains why people returning to malaria zones after time away are as vulnerable as first-time visitors.
wowFactor: Surviving malaria builds only a temporary immunity that vanishes without continued exposure — move away from a malaria zone and you lose your protection within years.

Immune — prior exposure built permanent resistance

statement: In 2023, malaria caused an estimated 597,000 deaths worldwide, with around 95% of cases and deaths occurring in sub-Saharan Africa.
quizQuestion: What percentage of malaria cases and deaths occur in sub-Saharan Africa?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2023 there were approximately 263 million malaria cases globally, resulting in an estimated 597,000 deaths. Around 95% of both cases and deaths occurred in sub-Saharan Africa, making it the most heavily affected region by far despite malaria being found across tropical and subtropical zones worldwide.
wowFactor: Malaria is a global disease — but 95% of its deaths are concentrated in one region: sub-Saharan Africa. Nearly 600,000 people died from it in 2023 alone.

statement: In 2024, tuberculosis caused 1.23 million deaths, making it the leading cause of death from any single infectious disease.
quizQuestion: Which disease killed the most people from a single infectious cause in 2024?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, TB caused an estimated 1.23 million deaths in 2024 and newly infected 10.7 million people that year — outpacing every other single infectious disease as a killer. This is striking because TB is both ancient and largely preventable.
wowFactor: TB — a disease known since ancient times — was still the world's deadliest single infectious disease killer in 2024, outpacing HIV and malaria.

Which disease was responsible for an estimated quarter of all European deaths in the 1800s?

statement: An estimated one quarter of the world's population — approximately 2 billion people — carry latent tuberculosis with no symptoms.
quizQuestion: What fraction of the world's population is estimated to carry latent TB?
explanation: Wikipedia states that roughly 2 billion people — one quarter of humanity — have latent TB, meaning the bacteria are present but the immune system holds them in check. Only a small proportion ever develop active disease, usually when the immune system weakens.
wowFactor: Right now, one in four people on Earth is silently carrying TB bacteria — they just don't know it because their immune system keeps it dormant.

statement: Tuberculosis is colloquially called the "white death" because the pallor it gives sufferers as it slowly wastes the body.
quizQuestion: What eerie nickname is tuberculosis also known by?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, TB earned the nickname 'the white death' from the extreme pallor it produces as it slowly destroys the body — a striking contrast to the 'Black Death' of plague. In the 19th century it was fashionably romanticized as giving victims a delicate, ethereal appearance.
wowFactor: TB was so common in 1800s Europe that its ghostly pallor was considered fashionably beautiful — the original 'consumption chic'.

statement: The World Health Organization maintains a list of essential medicines — the minimum drugs every functioning health system should have available.
quizQuestion: Which global body publishes the list of essential medicines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the WHO keeps a regularly updated list of essential medicines that defines the minimum pharmaceutical needs for a basic healthcare system. The list guides drug procurement in low-income countries and international aid programs.
wowFactor: The WHO's essential medicines list is basically the global 'you need at least these drugs to have a health system' checklist — and it shapes what billions of people can access.

statement: Medications are divided into prescription drugs — dispensed only on a doctor's order — and over-the-counter drugs that consumers can purchase themselves.
quizQuestion: What is the key division that separates prescription drugs from over-the-counter drugs?
explanation: Wikipedia explains that one of the most important ways drugs are classified is by 'level of control' — whether a pharmacist can dispense them only with a medical prescription or whether consumers can buy them freely. This system exists to prevent misuse of potent or addictive substances.
wowFactor: The dividing line between 'anyone can buy this' and 'doctor's note required' comes down to a single regulatory concept: level of control.

statement: The Spanish flu of 1918–1920 was the most severe influenza pandemic in recorded history, killing tens of millions worldwide.
quizQuestion: Which flu pandemic, starting in 1918, was the most severe in recorded history?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Spanish flu (1918–1920) was the most severe of the five flu pandemics since 1900. It killed vastly more people than the battlefield casualties of World War I, and uniquely struck young healthy adults hardest — the reverse of typical flu patterns.
wowFactor: The Spanish flu killed more people than World War I — and it did it in just two years, between 1918 and 1920.

statement: In a typical year, influenza causes up to 650,000 respiratory-related deaths globally.
quizQuestion: How many respiratory deaths does influenza cause globally each typical year?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, seasonal flu causes 3 to 5 million severe cases and up to 650,000 respiratory deaths every year worldwide — mostly in high-risk groups like young children, the elderly, and those with chronic conditions. Annual vaccination is the main preventive tool.
wowFactor: Regular old seasonal flu — not a pandemic — kills up to 650,000 people every single year, quietly, without major headlines.

statement: To date, seven cholera pandemics have occurred, with the most recent beginning in 1961 and still ongoing.
quizQuestion: How many global cholera pandemics have occurred in recorded history?
explanation: Wikipedia notes that seven cholera pandemics have struck the world, with the seventh — the most recent — beginning in 1961 and continuing to this day. This makes cholera unique among major infectious diseases in still being in an active pandemic phase.
wowFactor: The world is technically still in the seventh cholera pandemic — it started in 1961 and has never officially ended.

statement: Once rabies symptoms appear, death is virtually certain — as of 2016, only fourteen people had ever been documented to survive symptomatic rabies.
quizQuestion: How many people have ever been documented surviving rabies after symptoms appeared (as of 2016)?
explanation: Wikipedia states that as of 2016, only 14 people had ever been documented to survive a rabies infection after symptoms began. Once the virus reaches the central nervous system and causes encephalitis, the outcome is virtually always fatal — making timely post-exposure treatment critical.
wowFactor: Rabies is so deadly that in all of human history up to 2016, only 14 people survived it after symptoms appeared — fewer than the passengers on a minibus.

statement: The rabies virus's incubation time depends on how far it must travel along nerves to reach the brain — a bite on the foot takes longer to cause symptoms than a bite on the neck.
quizQuestion: What determines how long rabies takes to cause symptoms after a bite?
explanation: Wikipedia explains that rabies travels along peripheral nerves toward the central nervous system, and the incubation period — typically one to three months — depends on the physical distance the virus must cover. A bite to the face or neck can progress to symptoms much faster than a bite to a foot.
wowFactor: If a rabid animal bites your foot, you have more time to seek treatment than if it bites your face — because the virus literally has further to walk along your nerves to reach your brain.

statement: Ebola virus disease has an average mortality rate of approximately 50%, with a range of 25% to 90% depending on the viral species and timing of treatment.
quizQuestion: What is the average mortality rate of Ebola virus disease across outbreaks?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ebola mortality averages around 50% but swings widely from 25% to 90% depending on which of the four disease-causing ebolavirus species is involved and how quickly treatment begins. Death typically results from shock caused by fluid loss between 6 and 16 days after first symptoms.
wowFactor: Ebola can kill anywhere from 1 in 4 to 9 in 10 of those infected — the same disease, with wildly different outcomes depending on which strain and how fast you get help.

statement: After recovering from Ebola, semen or breast milk may continue to carry the virus for weeks to several months.
quizQuestion: After recovering from Ebola, what bodily fluids can still carry the virus for months?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, even after a person recovers from Ebola, the virus can persist in semen or breast milk for anywhere from several weeks to several months. This makes sexual contact and breastfeeding potential transmission routes long after the patient has clinically recovered.
wowFactor: Ebola survivors aren't completely safe to others — the virus can linger in semen and breast milk for months after full recovery.

statement: Common cold symptoms — runny nose, sore throat, sneezing — are mostly caused by the immune system's response, not direct damage from the virus itself.
quizQuestion: What actually causes most common cold symptoms like a runny nose and sore throat?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cold symptoms are 'mostly due to the body's immune response to the infection rather than to tissue destruction by the viruses themselves.' The runny nose, sneezing, and sore throat are your immune system fighting back — which is why you feel worse when you're recovering, not worse when the virus first arrives.
wowFactor: That miserable runny nose and sore throat you get with a cold? That's actually your own immune system attacking — the virus itself causes almost no direct damage.

statement: Leprosy is so weakly contagious that 95% of people exposed to the bacterium Mycobacterium leprae never develop the disease.
quizQuestion: What percentage of people exposed to leprosy never actually develop the disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, leprosy has a very low pathogenicity — 95% of people who encounter M. leprae never develop any disease whatsoever, protected by normal immune function. Only those with genetic susceptibility or compromised immunity are at significant risk, making forced segregation in leper colonies medically unjustifiable.
wowFactor: Leprosy — history's most feared disease — is so weakly contagious that 19 out of 20 people exposed to it never get sick at all.

statement: In the 20 years from 1994 to 2014, 16 million people worldwide were cured of leprosy using multidrug therapy.
quizQuestion: How many people were cured of leprosy globally between 1994 and 2014?
explanation: Wikipedia states that 16 million people were cured of leprosy in just 20 years (1994–2014) using multidrug therapy provided free of charge by the WHO. Active cases fell from 5.2 million in the 1980s to fewer than 200,000 by 2020 — one of public health's great success stories.
wowFactor: Leprosy — the disease that shaped social stigma for millennia — was quietly cured in 16 million people in just 20 years, yet almost nobody talks about it.

statement: Measles is so contagious that nine out of ten unvaccinated people sharing living space with an infected person will become infected.
quizQuestion: How many unvaccinated people sharing space with a measles patient will typically get infected?
explanation: Wikipedia states that measles is 'extremely contagious: nine out of ten people who are not immune and share living space with an infected person will be infected.' This is among the highest contagion rates of any human disease, which is why maintaining 95%+ vaccination coverage is essential for herd immunity.
wowFactor: Measles is so ferociously contagious that if you're unvaccinated and share a room with someone who has it, you have a 90% chance of catching it.

statement: The word 'vaccine' comes from the Latin for cowpox — Edward Jenner used cowpox infection to protect people against the far more dangerous smallpox.
quizQuestion: Which animal disease gave the word 'vaccine' its name?
explanation: Wikipedia explains that 'vaccine' derives from Variolae vaccinae ('smallpox of the cow') — the term Jenner coined for cowpox in his 1798 paper describing how cowpox exposure protected against smallpox. The word has since expanded to cover all protective inoculations, but its bovine origin is preserved in every dose ever given.
wowFactor: Every time someone gets a vaccine — for flu, COVID, or anything else — they're using a word that literally means 'from a cow', honouring Edward Jenner's cowpox experiment from 1798.

statement: The word 'antibiotic' literally means 'opposing life', from the Greek roots anti ('against') and bios ('life').
quizQuestion: What does the word 'antibiotic' literally mean in Greek?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'antibiotic' combines the Greek anti ('against') and bios ('life'). The name reflects that the drugs are produced by one living organism fighting another — originally fungi like Penicillium mold fighting bacteria.
wowFactor: Every time you take an antibiotic, you're literally ingesting something named 'opposing life' — a drug created by one microbe to kill another in a microscopic arms race.

statement: Antibiotics are powerless against viruses — they only kill or inhibit bacteria, not the viruses that cause colds or flu.
quizQuestion: Which type of pathogen are antibiotics completely ineffective against?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, antibiotics are only active against bacteria. Drugs that fight viruses are called antivirals, and drugs that fight fungi are called antifungals. This distinction matters clinically — prescribing antibiotics for a viral cold is both useless and contributes to resistance.
wowFactor: Most colds and flus are viral, so every antibiotic prescription for them is completely useless — yet overuse is a leading driver of antibiotic resistance worldwide.

statement: Antibiotic resistance is a recognized characteristic of the antibiotic class itself — built into their very nature as bacteria evolve to survive them.
quizQuestion: What built-in characteristic threatens to make antibiotics useless over time?
explanation: According to Wikidata, antibiotic resistance is listed as a defining characteristic of the antibiotic class. When bacteria are repeatedly exposed to antibiotics, natural selection favors strains that survive — those resistant bacteria multiply and spread, rendering the drugs less effective.
wowFactor: Resistance isn't a side effect or a mistake — it's baked into what antibiotics are. Every time bacteria survive a dose, evolution is quietly winning.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2024 approximately 50 million people have epilepsy worldwide. Nearly 80% of those cases occur in low- and middle-income countries, where access to treatment is limited, meaning most people with epilepsy live without adequate care.
wowFactor: 50 million people — more than the population of Spain — are living with epilepsy right now, yet most are in low-income countries with little access to treatment.

statement: The word 'epilepsy' comes from Ancient Greek meaning 'to seize, possess, or afflict' — reflecting ancient beliefs that seizures were supernatural possession.
quizQuestion: What does the Ancient Greek root of 'epilepsy' mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, epilepsy derives from the Ancient Greek epilambanein, meaning 'to seize, possess, or afflict.' This etymology reflects ancient interpretations of seizures as demonic possession — a belief that caused enormous historical stigma for people with epilepsy.
wowFactor: Epilepsy's name is a relic of ancient superstition — for millennia, seizures were thought to be gods or demons seizing control of a person's body.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, historical documentation of STIs dates back to at least the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) and the Hebrew Bible. The Ebers Papyrus is one of the oldest and most comprehensive medical texts from ancient Egypt, covering hundreds of conditions and treatments.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, asthma comes from the Greek ásthma meaning 'panting.' The disease was recognized as early as Ancient Egypt, making it one of the oldest described medical conditions — though ancient treatments bore little resemblance to modern inhalers.

statement: Vaccination works by exploiting the adaptive immune system's ability to remember pathogens it has previously encountered and mount a faster, stronger response next time.
quizQuestion: Which immune system property is the fundamental basis for how vaccines work?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the adaptive immune system creates immunological memory — an enhanced response against subsequent encounters with the same pathogen. This mechanism is the fundamental basis of vaccination: expose the immune system to a harmless version of a pathogen so it remembers how to fight the real thing.
wowFactor: Every vaccine ever made relies on a single biological trick: teaching your immune system to remember something it's never actually survived, so it's ready when the real threat arrives.

variants.question: True or false: the adaptive immune system tailors its response to each specific pathogen it encounters.
What type of immune system creates the immunological memory that vaccines rely on?
The adaptive immune system creates ___ memory, producing an enhanced response against repeat infections.
Why does a second infection with the same virus usually cause milder illness than the first?
variants.correctAnswer: The immune system already has memory of it
distractors: The virus mutates to be less dangerous
The body eliminates viruses faster with age
Antibiotics work better on familiar pathogens

visualDescription: A pixel art immune cell examining a wanted poster of a virus, then later instantly recognizing the same virus in a crowd and neutralizing it.

statement: The immune system can detect and respond to foreign objects as mundane as a wood splinter, not just pathogens like bacteria or viruses.
quizQuestion: Besides bacteria and viruses, what everyday object can trigger an immune response?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the immune system detects and responds to a wide variety of threats including viruses, bacteria, parasites, cancer cells, and foreign objects such as wood splinters — distinguishing them from the body's own healthy tissue. The inflammatory response around a splinter is literally the immune system doing its job.
wowFactor: That redness around a splinter isn't infection — it's your immune system waging war on a piece of wood, sending cells to isolate, attack, and expel the foreign object.

variants.question: True or false: the immune system can respond to non-biological foreign objects like splinters.
What bodily system causes the redness and swelling around a lodged wood splinter?
The immune system distinguishes foreign objects and pathogens from the body's own ___ tissue.
The immune system attacks a transplanted organ despite it being human tissue. What does this illustrate?
variants.correctAnswer: It distinguishes self from non-self tissue
distractors: The adaptive immune system makes errors

visualDescription: A pixel art finger with a tiny wood splinter embedded, surrounded by glowing immune cells converging on it from the bloodstream below.

statement: Autoimmunity occurs when a hyperactive immune system attacks the body's own healthy tissue as if it were a foreign invader.
quizQuestion: What happens when the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own healthy tissue?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, autoimmunity results from a hyperactive immune system attacking normal tissues as if they were foreign organisms. Common autoimmune diseases include Hashimoto's thyroiditis, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and systemic lupus erythematosus.
wowFactor: Rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and lupus are all the same kind of tragedy — the immune system, your body's greatest protector, turning on you.

variants.question: True or false: rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis, rheumatoid arthritis, and type 1 diabetes are all examples of which condition?
Immunodeficiency occurs when the immune system is ___ active than normal, causing recurring infections.
A patient's immune system attacks their pancreatic beta cells, destroying insulin production. What disease results?

visualDescription: A pixel art immune cell wearing armor aggressively attacking a healthy body cell, while confused bystander cells watch in horror.

statement: The word 'allergy' was first coined by Austrian physician Clemens von Pirquet in 1906.
quizQuestion: Who invented the word 'allergy' and in what year?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the word 'allergy' was first used by Clemens von Pirquet in 1906 — making it a surprisingly recent term for such a common condition. Von Pirquet was an Austrian pediatrician who also made major contributions to understanding tuberculosis and immune reactions.
wowFactor: The word 'allergy' is only about 120 years old — before 1906 there was no word for what millions of people experienced every hay fever season.

variants.question: True or false: the word 'allergy' has been used since at least the 18th century.
Austrian physician Clemens von Pirquet is credited with coining which term in 1906?
The mechanism of allergic reactions involves ___ E antibodies binding to an allergen.
A 19th-century physician notices seasonal sneezing in patients but has no word for it. What year did that word finally arrive?

visualDescription: A Victorian-era Austrian doctor at a writing desk coins the word 'allergy' in an ornate ledger, a sneezing patient visible through his window.

statement: In the developed world, about 20% of people are affected by allergic rhinitis (hay fever), and about 20% have or have had atopic dermatitis.
quizQuestion: Roughly what fraction of people in the developed world have allergic rhinitis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the developed world about 20% of people are affected by allergic rhinitis, food allergy affects 10% of adults and 8% of children, and about 20% have or have had atopic dermatitis. Allergy rates appear to be increasing globally.
wowFactor: 1 in 5 people in developed countries has hay fever — allergies are so common they're statistically normal, yet we still don't fully understand why the immune system overreacts to harmless pollen.

variants.question: True or false: food allergy affects roughly the same percentage of adults and children.

statement: Approximately 75% of polio cases are completely asymptomatic — most infected people never know they had it.
quizQuestion: What percentage of polio cases show no symptoms at all?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, approximately 75% of polio cases are asymptomatic; mild symptoms like sore throat and fever occur in some, while only a proportion develop severe outcomes like paralysis. This means the terrifying paralysis cases were just the visible tip of a much larger silent epidemic.
wowFactor: Three out of four people who catch polio never feel a thing — yet the virus can still silently spread to others, which is what made outbreaks so hard to control before vaccines.

variants.question: True or false: the majority of polio infections cause permanent paralysis.
Which infectious disease is famous for causing paralysis but actually has no symptoms in about 75% of cases?

statement: As of October 2023, only Pakistan and Afghanistan remain endemic for wild poliovirus — the last two countries on Earth yet to eliminate it.
quizQuestion: As of 2023, which are the only two countries still endemic for wild poliovirus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of October 2023, only Pakistan and Afghanistan remain endemic for wild poliovirus. Through global vaccination campaigns, polio incidence declined from an estimated 350,000 cases in 1988 to just 30 confirmed cases in 2022.
wowFactor: In 1988 polio paralyzed 350,000 people a year across 125 countries. By 2022 only 30 cases remained on Earth — confined to just 2 countries. We're on the edge of wiping out a disease that terrified the world.

variants.question: True or false: polio has been completely eradicated from every country in the world.

statement: The poliovirus was first identified in 1909 by Austrian immunologist Karl Landsteiner, though the disease had been depicted in ancient art for thousands of years.
quizQuestion: Who first identified the poliovirus in 1909?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the virus that causes polio was first identified in 1909 by Austrian immunologist Karl Landsteiner. The disease itself had existed for thousands of years — depicted in ancient Egyptian art — but its viral cause was unknown until the 20th century. Landsteiner is also famous for discovering blood types.
wowFactor: Karl Landsteiner identified the poliovirus in 1909 — the same scientist who also discovered human blood types. One man, two world-changing discoveries.

variants.question: True or false: polio was first recognized as a distinct condition by an English physician in 1789.

variants.correctAnswer: Polio has existed for thousands of years
distractors: The virus was more severe in antiquity

statement: Syphilis deaths fell from 202,000 in 1990 to about 107,000 in 2015 — largely due to the availability of penicillin since the 1940s.
quizQuestion: How many deaths did syphilis cause globally in 2015?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, syphilis caused about 107,000 deaths in 2015, down from 202,000 in 1990. This dramatic decline followed the widespread availability of penicillin beginning in the 1940s. However, rates of infection have been rising again since the turn of the millennium in many countries.
wowFactor: Penicillin halved syphilis deaths in just decades — but rates are climbing again globally since 2000, showing that curable doesn't mean controlled.

variants.question: True or false: syphilis rates have been consistently falling since penicillin became available.

statement: The primary stage of syphilis classically presents with a single firm, painless, non-itchy skin ulcer called a chancre.
quizQuestion: What is the name of the painless ulcer that marks the primary stage of syphilis?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the primary stage of syphilis classically presents with a single chancre — a firm, painless, non-itchy skin ulceration usually between 1 and 2 cm in diameter. Because it's painless, it can go unnoticed, allowing the disease to progress undetected to more dangerous stages.
wowFactor: The first sign of syphilis is a painless sore — which is exactly why so many people missed it for centuries, letting the disease silently advance to attack the heart and brain.

variants.question: True or false: the primary syphilis ulcer (chancre) is typically painful.

statement: Since the varicella vaccine was introduced in the US in 1995, chickenpox infections have decreased by nearly 90%.
quizQuestion: By what percentage did US chickenpox infections fall after the varicella vaccine's 1995 introduction?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, since its introduction in 1995 in the United States, the varicella vaccine has resulted in a decrease in cases of nearly 90%. In 2013 there were 140 million cases of chickenpox and shingles worldwide, but before routine immunization the annual case count was similar to the number of births each year.
wowFactor: One vaccine introduced in 1995 cut US chickenpox cases by nearly 90% in a generation — a disease so common it once infected virtually every child is now rare.

statement: Chickenpox was not medically distinguished from smallpox until the late 19th century — for hundreds of years they were considered the same disease.
quizQuestion: Until which century was chickenpox not separated from smallpox as a distinct disease?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, chickenpox was not separated from smallpox until the late 19th century, despite the term 'chicken pox' first appearing in 1658. For roughly two centuries, doctors and patients confused the two diseases — one of which killed 30% of those it infected, while the other was generally mild.
wowFactor: For 200+ years, doctors couldn't tell chickenpox from smallpox — one a mild itchy inconvenience, the other a disfiguring killer. The first use of 'chicken pox' dates all the way back to 1658.

statement: HIV was discovered in 1983, yet it kills CD4+ T cells — the very immune cells needed to fight it — making AIDS a progressive collapse of the immune system.
quizQuestion: In what year was HIV first discovered?
explanation: According to Wikidata, HIV's time of discovery was 1983. The virus attacks CD4+ T cells — the immune system's command center — progressively destroying the very mechanism needed to fight it. Without treatment, average survival after infection is estimated at 9 to 11 years.
wowFactor: HIV's most diabolical trick is targeting CD4+ T cells — the exact immune cells your body would normally deploy to defeat any infection, including HIV itself.

statement: HIV is a lentivirus — a subgroup of retroviruses — meaning it uses reverse transcriptase to write its RNA genome into the host's DNA.
quizQuestion: HIV belongs to which subgroup of retroviruses, known for causing slow-progressing diseases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Human immunodeficiency viruses are two species of Lentivirus (a subgroup of retrovirus). The name lentivirus comes from the Latin for 'slow' — these viruses are characterized by a long incubation period and slow disease progression, which is why HIV takes years to cause AIDS without treatment.
wowFactor: HIV is called a 'slow virus' — a lentivirus — because it takes years to cause AIDS, silently dismantling your immune system while you feel fine. Its patience is what makes it so dangerous.

variants.question: True or false: HIV is classified as a retrovirus.

statement: Aspirin's active precursor, salicin, comes from willow tree bark and has been used medicinally for at least 2,400 years.
quizQuestion: Which tree's bark contains a natural precursor to aspirin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, salicin found in the bark of willow trees (genus Salix) is metabolized in the human gut into salicylic acid, which has medicinal properties. Humans have exploited this natural remedy for at least 2,400 years before the synthetic version — aspirin — was developed.
wowFactor: The painkiller in your medicine cabinet traces directly to an ancient remedy: people were chewing willow bark for the same effect 2,400 years ago!

statement: Unlike other NSAIDs, aspirin also suppresses normal platelet functioning, making it useful for preventing blood clots.
quizQuestion: What unique effect sets aspirin apart from other anti-inflammatory drugs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, aspirin works similarly to other NSAIDs for pain and inflammation, but uniquely suppresses normal platelet function. This antithrombotic property makes it useful long-term for preventing heart attacks, ischaemic strokes, and blood clots in high-risk patients.
wowFactor: Aspirin does something no other common painkiller can: it permanently disables platelets so your blood is less likely to clot — one reason doctors prescribe it daily after a heart attack.

variants.question: True or false: aspirin is used long-term to help prevent heart attacks and strokes in high-risk patients.

statement: Dengue fever is spreading from tropical regions into the Iberian Peninsula and southern United States, partly due to climate change.
quizQuestion: Where is dengue fever newly spreading beyond its traditional tropical range?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, dengue is already endemic in more than one hundred countries, and is now spreading into the Iberian Peninsula and the southern states of the US — regions that were previously too temperate for the Aedes mosquito. Climate change is partly attributed as a driving factor in this expansion.
wowFactor: Dengue — once confined to the tropics — is now showing up in Spain and the southern US, as warming temperatures push its mosquito host into new territory.

statement: Vaccination eradicated smallpox by 1979 — a disease that had already killed an estimated 300–500 million people in the 20th century alone.
quizQuestion: How many people did smallpox kill in the 20th century before vaccination eradicated it?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, smallpox was a contagious and deadly disease that killed 20–60% of infected adults and over 80% of infected children. When it was finally eradicated in 1979 through global vaccination campaigns, it had already killed an estimated 300–500 million people in the 20th century alone — making vaccination one of history's greatest life-saving achievements.
wowFactor: Vaccination wiped out a disease that killed up to 500 million people in just one century — more than both World Wars combined.

statement: The word 'vaccination' comes from the Latin word for cow ('vacca'), because the first vaccine was derived from a virus that infects cows.
quizQuestion: Why is the word 'vaccination' derived from the Latin word for cow?
correctAnswer: The first vaccine came from a cow virus
distractors: Cows were first to be vaccinated
Early vaccines were tested on cattle
The needle resembled a cow horn
Cow milk was used as the vaccine base
Cows were immune to all diseases
The inventor kept cows on his farm
Cowpox symptoms resemble a cow's hide pattern
Cow bile was mixed with the vaccine
explanation: According to Wikipedia, immunization was called 'vaccination' because it was derived from a virus affecting cows — cowpox. The smallpox vaccine, invented by Edward Jenner in 1796, used this bovine virus, giving the entire field of vaccination its name from the Latin 'vacca' meaning cow.
wowFactor: Every vaccine ever given owes its name to cows — because Edward Jenner discovered the first one by using a virus that infected cow udders!

statement: Only about 5 to 10 percent of breast cancer cases result from an inherited genetic predisposition, such as BRCA mutations — the vast majority arise from non-inherited factors.
quizQuestion: What percentage of breast cancers are caused by inherited genetic mutations like BRCA?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only about 5–10% of breast cancer cases are the result of an inherited genetic predisposition, including BRCA mutations. The majority of cases arise from non-inherited risk factors such as obesity, lack of exercise, alcohol consumption, and age.
wowFactor: Despite all the media focus on the BRCA gene, inherited mutations only cause about 1 in 10 breast cancers — most cases arise from lifestyle and environmental factors, not family genes.

statement: Infectious diseases caused approximately 9.2 million deaths in 2013, representing 17% of all deaths globally that year.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, infectious diseases remain a significant global health concern, causing approximately 9.2 million deaths in 2013 — which represented 17% of all deaths that year. The branch of medicine that focuses on infections is referred to as infectious diseases.
wowFactor: Despite all of modern medicine's advances, infections still killed 9.2 million people in a single year — nearly 1 in 6 of all deaths worldwide.

statement: In first-world countries, children under 15 with leukemia have a five-year survival rate greater than 60%, and up to 90% depending on the type.
quizQuestion: What is the five-year survival rate range for children under 15 with leukemia in wealthy countries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the five-year survival rate for leukemia in the US was 68% overall (2015–2021). For children under 15 in first-world countries, the rate climbs to greater than 60% and can reach 90% depending on the type of leukemia — a dramatic improvement from earlier eras when most cases were fatal.
wowFactor: Childhood leukemia, once almost always fatal, now has survival rates up to 90% in developed countries — one of medicine's most dramatic turnarounds.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2021, COPD affected about 213 million people worldwide and was the fourth-biggest cause of death, responsible for approximately 5% of total deaths. Almost 90% of COPD deaths in those under 70 occur in low and middle income countries, highlighting a profound global health inequality.
wowFactor: COPD kills enough people to rank as the world's fourth-biggest cause of death — and 9 out of 10 of its victims under 70 live in poorer countries.

statement: Even with treatment, only around 20% of lung cancer patients survive five years from their diagnosis.
quizQuestion: What percentage of lung cancer patients survive five years even with treatment?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, even with treatment, only around 20% of lung cancer patients survive five years from their diagnosis. Survival rates are higher for those diagnosed at an earlier stage, diagnosed at a younger age, and in women compared to men. Quitting smoking both reduces the chance of developing lung cancer and improves outcomes in those already diagnosed.
wowFactor: Lung cancer's five-year survival rate is just 20% even with treatment — which is why catching it early, before it spreads, is so critically important.

statement: Typhoid fever only infects humans — Salmonella Typhi has no known animal reservoirs, making contaminated human feces the sole source of transmission.
quizQuestion: What makes typhoid fever unusual compared to most bacterial diseases?
correctAnswer: It only infects humans, with no animal reservoir
distractors: It can survive outside the body for decades
It is spread only by insect bites
Animals transmit it but cannot get sick from it
It mutates too fast for vaccines to work
It can only spread through sexual contact
It is transmitted by birds specifically
Antibiotics have no effect on it
It lives exclusively in salt water
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Salmonella enterica Typhi is believed to infect and replicate only within humans, with no known animal reservoirs. Typhoid is spread by ingesting food or water contaminated with the feces of an infected person. This makes contaminated drinking water and poor sanitation the primary risk factors globally.
wowFactor: Unlike most dangerous bacteria, typhoid's pathogen lives ONLY in humans — meaning the only way to get typhoid is from another infected human, usually through contaminated water.
