statement: A typical scabies infection involves only 10 to 15 mites on the entire body — yet these few mites cause intense, whole-body itching through an allergic reaction.
quizQuestion: How many typically cause a standard scabies infection?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a typical scabies infection involves only between 10 and 15 mites on the entire body. The intense itching is not caused by the mites themselves tearing the skin, but by an allergic reaction to the mites' presence. The mites are only 0.2–0.45 mm, at the very limit of human vision.
wowFactor: Scabies — with its maddening full-body itch — is caused by as few as 10 tiny mites. Your immune system's overreaction to those 10 mites does all the damage.

statement: Gonorrhea, colloquially known as 'the clap', causes an estimated 33 to 106 million new cases globally every year.
quizQuestion: How many new gonorrhea cases occur globally each year, approximately?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, an estimated 33 to 106 million new gonorrhea cases occur each year globally. The disease is caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and many of those infected have no symptoms — making undetected transmission common. Antibiotic resistance is a growing treatment challenge.
wowFactor: Up to 106 million new gonorrhea infections happen every single year worldwide — yet many carriers have no symptoms at all, making it one of history's most stealthy diseases.

statement: About one-third of people infected with mumps never develop any symptoms, yet can still spread the virus.
quizQuestion: What fraction of mumps infections are completely asymptomatic?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, about one-third of people with a mumps infection do not have any symptoms. Despite being asymptomatic, transmission can occur from one week before onset of symptoms through eight days after — making silent spreaders a key challenge in controlling outbreaks.
wowFactor: A third of people with mumps never feel sick at all — but they're still contagious, silently spreading the virus to others who won't be so lucky.

statement: In severe diphtheria, a tough grey or white membrane called a pseudomembrane grows in the throat and can slowly block the airway, causing suffocation.
quizQuestion: What dangerous structure forms in the throat during severe diphtheria?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in severe diphtheria cases a grey or white patch called a pseudomembrane develops in the throat. This tough, leathery membrane slowly blocks the airway, starting with a barking cough and potentially leading to strangulation and death if untreated. Diphtheria cases fell from nearly 100,000 in 1980 to just 4,500 in 2015 due to widespread vaccination.
wowFactor: Diphtheria grows a literal suffocating membrane inside your throat — a leathery film that slowly chokes off the airway if left untreated. Vaccination made this nightmare nearly extinct.

variants.question: True or false: the diphtheria pseudomembrane can grow to cover a wide area of the throat and cause suffocation.

statement: Tetanus, nicknamed 'lockjaw', is caused by Clostridium tetani bacteria found in ordinary soil, dust, and manure — entering the body through even small cuts.
quizQuestion: Where is the tetanus-causing bacterium Clostridium tetani commonly found?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Clostridium tetani is commonly found in soil, saliva, dust, and manure. The bacteria generally enter through a break in the skin such as a cut or puncture wound. They produce toxins that interfere with normal muscle contractions, causing the characteristic spasms — with jaw spasms ('lockjaw') typically occurring first.
wowFactor: Tetanus lives in ordinary garden soil — a simple rusty nail scratch is enough for its toxin to reach your nervous system and lock your jaw shut.

variants.question: True or false: tetanus does not spread between people — it enters through wounds from the environment.

statement: Cancer was first described in ancient Egypt around 3000 BCE, when the Edwin Smith Papyrus described a 'bulging tumor of the breast.'
quizQuestion: Which ancient document contains the earliest known description of a tumor, dating to around 3000 BCE?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cancer was first found described in humans in the years 3000 BCE in Egypt. The Edwin Smith Papyrus described a form of cancer as 'a bulging tumor of the breast.' The word 'oncology' itself comes from the Greek 'onkos' (tumor), first used by the Roman physician Galen around 130–200 CE.
wowFactor: Humans have been fighting cancer for at least 5,000 years — the oldest written description of a tumor was carved into an Egyptian papyrus in 3000 BCE.

statement: Over 95% of human anthrax infections are the skin (cutaneous) form, which presents as a painless black-centered ulcer.
quizQuestion: What percentage of human anthrax cases are the cutaneous (skin) form?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, skin infections represent more than 95% of all human anthrax cases. Cutaneous anthrax presents as a small blister that often turns into a painless ulcer with a characteristic black center. Without treatment, the risk of death from skin anthrax is 23.7%, far lower than the 50–80% mortality of respiratory anthrax.
wowFactor: While movies focus on weaponized inhalation anthrax, over 95% of actual human cases are skin infections — identifiable by a distinctive painless black sore.

statement: In cirrhosis, the liver's healthy tissue is replaced by permanent scar tissue — a process that cannot currently be reversed, only slowed.
quizQuestion: In liver cirrhosis, healthy liver is replaced by what irreversible substance?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cirrhosis occurs when normal functioning liver tissue (parenchyma) is replaced by scar tissue (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules as a result of chronic liver disease. Over time, scar tissue increases resistance to blood flow, causing portal hypertension and impairing liver function. No cure currently exists — only treatments that slow progression.
wowFactor: The liver is one of the body's most regenerative organs, but in cirrhosis it loses that superpower — replaced by permanent scar tissue that medicine still cannot undo.

statement: Ilya Mechnikov discovered phagocytosis — how the body engulfs and destroys invaders — by observing unusual cells surrounding thorns he stabbed into starfish larvae.
quizQuestion: How did Ilya Mechnikov first observe phagocytosis, the body's pathogen-engulfing defense?
correctAnswer: Watching cells surround thorns in starfish larvae
distractors: Observing white blood cells in human wounds
Studying immune cells in frog eggs
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ilya Mechnikov pinned small thorns into starfish larvae and noticed unusual cells surrounding the thorns — the body's active response to maintain its integrity. He was the first to observe phagocytosis in this way, and received the Nobel Prize in 1908 alongside Paul Ehrlich for their work on immunity.
wowFactor: The discovery that your immune system can eat invaders alive came from a scientist poking thorns into starfish babies and watching what happened — science at its most wonderfully unexpected.

variants.question: True or false: Ilya Mechnikov won the Nobel Prize in 1908 for his work on immunity alongside Paul Ehrlich.

statement: Dermatology covers not only rashes and cosmetic issues, but life-threatening emergencies like necrotizing fasciitis and Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
quizQuestion: Which gruesome life-threatening condition falls under the specialty of dermatology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, dermatology encompasses not only routine inflammatory diseases, infections, hair loss, and cosmetic issues, but also life-threatening emergencies such as serious burns, angioedema, necrotizing fasciitis (flesh-eating disease), and Stevens-Johnson syndrome — a severe drug reaction that destroys skin from the inside out.
wowFactor: Dermatology isn't just treating acne — it also covers necrotizing fasciitis, the 'flesh-eating disease' that can kill within days if not treated as an emergency.

statement: The distinction between 'epidemic' (widespread outbreak) and 'endemic' (constantly present) disease was first drawn by Hippocrates over 2,000 years ago.
quizQuestion: Who first distinguished between 'epidemic' and 'endemic' diseases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the distinction between 'epidemic' and 'endemic' was first drawn by Hippocrates. The term 'epidemiology' itself appears to have first been used to describe the study of epidemics in 1802 by the Spanish physician Joaquín de Villalba. Epidemiology remains a cornerstone of public health, shaping policy decisions and identifying risk factors for disease.
wowFactor: Hippocrates invented the concept of 'epidemic' vs 'endemic' over 2,000 years ago — the same language doctors still use today to track outbreaks.

statement: Dental cavities are caused by bacteria that produce acid when they break down sugar on tooth surfaces, dissolving the hard tissues of the teeth.
quizQuestion: What do bacteria produce that directly causes tooth decay?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the bacteria Streptococcus mutans and Lactobacillus break down sugars and produce acid as a byproduct — this acid then dissolves enamel, dentin, and cementum. The simpler the sugar, the faster the acid attack.
wowFactor: Every time you eat sugar, mouth bacteria throw an acid party on your teeth — the 'cavities' are just the aftermath of that feast!

statement: As of 2016, approximately 3.6 billion people — nearly half the world's population — had dental caries in their permanent teeth.
quizQuestion: How many people worldwide had permanent tooth decay as of 2016?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 3.6 billion people (48% of the world's population) had dental caries in their permanent teeth as of 2016. The WHO estimates nearly all adults experience dental cavities at some point in their lives — making it perhaps the most widespread disease on Earth.
wowFactor: Tooth decay affects nearly half of all humans alive today — it may be the single most common disease on the planet!

statement: Fluoride prevents dental cavities by helping rebuild tooth minerals faster than bacteria can dissolve them, and can be obtained from water, salt, or toothpaste.
quizQuestion: What mineral strengthens teeth against acid produced by bacteria?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fluoride can be acquired from water, salt, or toothpaste and works by helping rebuild minerals in tooth enamel — specifically making the enamel more resistant to acid attack. This is why fluoridated water dramatically reduced cavities in the 20th century.
wowFactor: Fluoride in your tap water has been quietly saving billions of teeth for decades — one of the most cost-effective public health interventions ever discovered!

statement: Jonas Salk deliberately chose not to patent his polio vaccine in 1955, giving up enormous potential profits to maximize its global distribution.
quizQuestion: Why did Jonas Salk choose not to patent his polio vaccine?
distractors: He donated it to a charity
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Salk chose not to patent the vaccine or seek any profit from it to maximize its global distribution. When asked who owned the patent, Salk famously replied the people did. His patent attorney also noted that his techniques were not novel enough to hold a patent anyway.
wowFactor: Salk could have become a billionaire from the polio vaccine — he gave it away for free to the world, calling universal vaccination a 'moral commitment'!

statement: Less than 25 years after Salk's polio vaccine was released, domestic transmission of polio had been completely eliminated in the United States.
quizQuestion: How long after Salk's vaccine did the US eliminate domestic polio transmission?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, less than 25 years after the release of Salk's vaccine, domestic transmission of polio had been eliminated in the United States. A complementary oral vaccine was developed by Albert Sabin, coming into commercial use in 1961, and by 1959 the Salk vaccine had reached about 90 countries.
wowFactor: Polio used to paralyze thousands of American children every summer — within a generation of Salk's vaccine, it was completely wiped out in the US!

statement: In 1927, yellow fever became the first human virus ever to be isolated by scientists.
quizQuestion: Which disease's virus was the first ever isolated from a human host?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1927 the yellow fever virus became the first human virus to be isolated — a landmark achievement that opened the door to modern virology. The disease had devastated cities across the Americas and Africa for centuries before this breakthrough.
wowFactor: Yellow fever was so deadly it terrified 18th-century cities — and its virus holds the honor of being the very first human virus ever isolated by science!

statement: Osteoarthritis is the most common form of arthritis, affecting more than 3.8% of people, while rheumatoid arthritis — an autoimmune disorder — affects only about 0.24%.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disorder where the body attacks its own joint tissue, commonly affecting the hands and feet. Osteoarthritis, by contrast, is caused by physical wear over time and is far more common, especially in weight-bearing joints like hips and knees.
wowFactor: In rheumatoid arthritis, your own immune system mistakes your joints for the enemy — it's your body fighting itself, not just wear and tear!

statement: Unlike the closed circulatory system, the lymphatic system is open — lymph fluid begins as blood plasma that leaks out of capillaries into body tissues.
quizQuestion: How does the lymphatic system differ from the circulatory system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the circulatory system is closed — blood never leaves the vessels — while the lymphatic system is open, beginning with fluid that leaks out of blood capillaries into the surrounding tissues. The system returns about 3 litres of this fluid per day back to the bloodstream.
wowFactor: Your lymph fluid starts as blood plasma that literally leaks out of your veins — the lymphatic system is basically your body's clean-up crew collecting what the blood leaves behind!

statement: The circulatory system processes about 20 litres of blood through capillary filtration each day, but only 17 litres are directly reabsorbed — the remaining 3 litres drain into the lymphatic system.
quizQuestion: How many litres of blood plasma does the circulatory system filter through capillaries each day?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the circulatory system processes an average of 20 litres of blood per day through capillary filtration. About 17 litres return directly to blood vessels, and the remaining 3 litres are collected by the lymphatic system and returned via lymph ducts to the subclavian veins.
wowFactor: Your blood leaks 3 litres of fluid into your tissues every single day — your lymphatic system is constantly on call to collect it all and return it to your bloodstream!

statement: Hematology is the branch of medicine focused on blood disorders, covering diseases from hemophilia and sickle cell anemia to leukemia, multiple myeloma, and lymphoma.
quizQuestion: What medical specialty studies and treats diseases of the blood?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hematology covers the cause, prognosis, treatment, and prevention of diseases related to blood and its components — including blood cells, hemoglobin, bone marrow, platelets, and the coagulation mechanism. The field treats conditions ranging from bleeding disorders like hemophilia to blood cancers like leukemia.
wowFactor: Everything from a tiny bruise that won't stop bleeding to blood cancers that affect millions — all of it falls under one medical specialty: hematology!

statement: The word 'pathogen' comes from Greek roots meaning 'suffering' and 'producer of' — it came into common use in the 1880s during the golden age of germ theory.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term pathogen came into use in the 1880s, derived from the Greek pathos (suffering) and -genes (producer of). This coincided with Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch's groundbreaking work establishing germ theory — the revolutionary idea that microorganisms cause disease.
wowFactor: The word 'pathogen' only entered science in the 1880s — before that, most people still believed disease came from 'bad air', not invisible germs!

explanation: According to Wikipedia, the invention of hormone therapies for prostate cancer was recognized with the 1966 Nobel Prize to Charles Huggins. statement: High-risk HPV strains cause about 5% of all cancers worldwide, including cervical, oral, anal, and genital cancers.
quizQuestion: What fraction of all cancers worldwide are caused by high-risk HPV strains?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, high-risk HPVs cause about 5% of all cancers worldwide. HPV16 alone is responsible for almost 90% of HPV-related cancers of the mouth, throat, or tonsils, and between 60% and 90% of anal, genital, and cervical cancers are also linked to HPV.
wowFactor: One virus — HPV — is responsible for 5% of every cancer on Earth, including most cervical cancers and the majority of throat cancers. And there's a vaccine for it!

statement: Colorectal cancer typically starts as a benign polyp in the colon — a growth that slowly becomes cancerous over years, which is why colonoscopy screening can prevent deaths by catching and removing polyps early.
quizQuestion: What benign growth commonly transforms into colorectal cancer over time?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, colorectal cancer typically starts as a benign tumor, often in the form of a polyp, which over time becomes cancerous. During colonoscopy, small polyps may be removed if found — making screening a genuinely life-saving intervention that prevents cancer before it starts.
wowFactor: Colorectal cancer doesn't appear from nowhere — it starts as an innocent polyp you could remove in minutes during a routine colonoscopy, years before it turns deadly!

statement: After a herpes simplex infection, the virus travels along sensory nerves to nerve cell bodies where it hides for life — becoming reactivated by stress, sunlight, or weakened immunity.
quizQuestion: Where does the herpes virus hide in the body to persist lifelong after infection?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after infection the herpes viruses are transported along sensory nerves to the nerve cell bodies, where they reside lifelong. Recurrence is triggered by decreased immune function, stress, or sunlight exposure — which is why cold sores tend to appear during illness or stressful periods.
wowFactor: Herpes doesn't just infect your skin — it retreats into your nerve cells and hides there for the rest of your life, waiting to be reactivated by stress or sunlight!

statement: The human heart has four chambers — two atria and two ventricles — and beats at a resting rate close to 72 beats per minute in adults.
quizQuestion: What is the approximate resting heart rate of an adult human?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the adult heart beats at a resting rate close to 72 beats per minute. The heart is divided into four chambers — upper left and right atria and lower left and right ventricles — and is located in the mediastinum, the middle compartment of the chest between the lungs.
wowFactor: Your heart beats about 72 times every minute — that's over 100,000 beats a day, without ever taking a rest, for your entire lifetime!

statement: Blood plasma makes up 55% of blood fluid and is itself 92% water by volume — it carries proteins, glucose, mineral ions, and hormones throughout the body.
quizQuestion: What percentage of blood fluid is made up of plasma?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, plasma constitutes 55% of blood fluid and is mostly water (92% by volume). It is the liquid matrix in which red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets are suspended — carrying nutrients, hormones, and waste products throughout the circulatory system.
wowFactor: More than half of your blood is actually just salty yellow water — plasma is essentially the delivery truck that carries all the important cargo!

statement: At any given moment, nearly 70% of the total blood volume in the human body is held within the veins — earning them the name 'capacitance vessels'.
quizQuestion: What percentage of total body blood volume is held in the veins at any time?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, veins can hold nearly 70% of total blood volume because they have thinner walls and wider lumens than arteries, allowing them to expand and store much more blood. This greater capacity is why they are called capacitance vessels.
wowFactor: Arteries get all the fame, but it's actually your veins that hold most of your blood — nearly 70% of your total blood volume sits in your veins right now!

statement: The number of people worldwide with diabetes surged from 200 million in 1990 to 828 million in 2024 — a more than fourfold increase in just 34 years.
quizQuestion: How many people worldwide had diabetes in 2024, up from 200 million in 1990?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the number of people diagnosed with diabetes has increased sharply from 200 million in 1990 to 828 million in 2024. Type 2 diabetes accounts for more than 95% of all cases, and diabetes now affects one in seven of the adult human population — exceeding earlier projections for 2045.
wowFactor: Diabetes cases more than quadrupled in just 34 years — from 200 million in 1990 to 828 million in 2024. It's one of the fastest-growing health crises on Earth!

statement: 'Broken heart syndrome' — also known as Takotsubo syndrome — is a real medical condition where extreme emotional stress can trigger a heart attack-like event.
quizQuestion: What is the medical name for 'broken heart ' triggered by extreme emotional stress?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, myocardial infarctions can be caused by coronary artery spasms triggered by significant emotional stress — often known as Takotsubo syndrome or broken heart syndrome. Cocaine use and extreme cold are other non-atherosclerotic triggers for MI.
wowFactor: Dying of a broken heart is medically real — extreme grief or emotional shock can trigger Takotsubo syndrome, causing heart attack-like damage with no blocked arteries!

statement: Vertebrates have a closed circulatory system where blood never leaves the blood vessels — unlike many invertebrates such as arthropods, whose blood (hemolymph) flows freely through body cavities.
quizQuestion: Which animal group has an open circulatory system where blood flows through body cavities?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the circulatory system is closed in vertebrates — meaning blood never leaves the network of blood vessels. Many invertebrates such as arthropods (insects, crabs, spiders) have an open circulatory system where a heart pumps hemolymph that returns via the body cavity rather than through blood vessels.
wowFactor: Insects don't have blood like you do — their hemolymph just sloshes freely around inside their body rather than flowing through dedicated blood vessels!

statement: The spleen converts hemoglobin from worn-out red blood cells into bilirubin, which is then removed by the liver.
quizQuestion: What does the spleen convert hemoglobin into after recycling old red blood cells?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the spleen metabolizes hemoglobin from senescent red blood cells: the globin portion is broken into amino acids, and the heme portion becomes bilirubin, which the liver then removes. This is why liver or spleen problems can cause jaundice.
wowFactor: The yellow pigment that causes jaundice is actually made by your spleen as it breaks down old blood cells — your body's recycling plant in action.

statement: About half of all people with high blood pressure do not know they have it, because it usually causes no symptoms.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, high blood pressure affects 33% of the global population, yet about half of those people are unaware of their condition because hypertension usually causes no symptoms. In 2019 it was a factor in 19% of all deaths worldwide (10.4 million people).
wowFactor: High blood pressure kills over 10 million people a year — and roughly half of those who have it don't even know it's happening inside them.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, up to three medications taken concurrently can control blood pressure in 90% of people. Treatment of moderately high arterial blood pressure (above 160/100 mmHg) with medications is associated with improved life expectancy.

statement: The human body produces approximately 2.4 million new red blood cells every second.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, approximately 2.4 million new erythrocytes are produced per second in human adults. Red blood cells circulate for about 100–120 days before their components are recycled by macrophages, primarily in the spleen.
wowFactor: Your body is manufacturing 2.4 million fresh red blood cells every single second — that's 144 million per minute, non-stop for your entire life.

statement: Iron-deficiency anemia is the most common blood disorder in the world, affecting nearly one billion people.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, anemia affects about one-fifth to one-third of the global population, and iron-deficiency anemia is its most common cause, affecting nearly one billion people. In 2013 it caused about 183,000 deaths — down from 213,000 in 1990.
wowFactor: Iron-deficiency anemia — treatable with a simple supplement — is sapping the energy of nearly 1 billion people right now, making it the world's most widespread blood disorder.

statement: White blood cells make up only about 1% of blood volume, yet that tiny fraction is what stands between you and infection.

wowFactor: Your entire immune defense — every pathogen-fighting soldier in your blood — is crammed into just 1% of your blood volume. Red cells hog the rest.

statement: White blood cells fall into three main subtypes: granulocytes, lymphocytes, and monocytes — all produced in the bone marrow.

All are derived from hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow.
wowFactor: Your immune army has three distinct divisions — granulocytes, lymphocytes, and monocytes — each fighting infections by completely different methods.

statement: Shigella bacteria cause about 165 million cases of diarrhea and 1.1 million deaths per year, almost all in the developing world.

statement: The standard method for measuring blood pressure — listening through a stethoscope as a cuff is released — was invented by Nikolai Korotkov.
quizQuestion: Who invented the auscultatory method of measuring blood pressure with a stethoscope?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Nikolai Korotkov is the discoverer/inventor of the blood pressure measurement technique. The method — known as auscultation — involves listening with a stethoscope to the characteristic sounds (Korotkov sounds) as arterial blood flow resumes under a released cuff. It remains the gold standard for non-invasive measurement.
wowFactor: Every blood pressure reading you've ever had relies on a 100-year-old technique invented by a single Russian surgeon — Nikolai Korotkov — who discovered those tell-tale artery sounds.

statement: A healthy person can lose 10–15% of their total blood volume without serious medical difficulties — comparable to donating blood.
quizQuestion: What percentage of blood volume can a healthy person lose without serious medical difficulty?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a healthy person can endure a loss of 10–15% of total blood volume without serious medical difficulties. By comparison, a blood donation typically takes 8–10% of the donor's blood volume, which is why donors generally feel fine afterward.
wowFactor: Donating blood takes about 8–10% of your blood volume — well within the 10–15% your body can lose without breaking a sweat. Your body just makes more.

statement: The pulse can be felt at any artery near the body's surface, including the neck, wrist, groin, behind the knee, and on the foot.
quizQuestion: At how many named body locations can a pulse be felt by pressing on a surface artery?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the pulse may be palpated at any point where an artery can be compressed near the body surface — including the neck (carotid), wrist (radial or ulnar), groin (femoral), behind the knee (popliteal), near the ankle (posterior tibial), and foot (dorsalis pedis). Adults are typically checked at the wrist or neck.
wowFactor: Your pulse is accessible at six named spots across your body — wrist, neck, groin, knee, ankle, and foot — every one a window into your heartbeat.

statement: Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels in the body, only 5 to 10 micrometres in diameter — thinner than a human hair.
quizQuestion: How wide are capillaries, the body's smallest blood vessels?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, capillaries are only 5 to 10 micrometres in diameter — a human hair is about 70 micrometres wide, making capillaries at least 7 times thinner. Their walls are just a single layer of endothelial cells, which is how oxygen, glucose, and carbon dioxide cross between blood and tissue.
wowFactor: Capillaries are so thin — just 5 to 10 micrometres — that red blood cells must squeeze through them in single file, like cars in a one-lane tunnel.

statement: Medical shock has four main types based on cause: hypovolemic, cardiogenic, obstructive, and distributive.
quizQuestion: How many main of medical shock are defined by their underlying cause?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, shock is divided into four main types: hypovolemic (low blood volume, e.g. from bleeding), cardiogenic (heart pump failure, e.g. heart attack), obstructive (blocked blood flow, e.g. cardiac tamponade), and distributive (abnormal blood vessel dilation, e.g. sepsis or anaphylaxis).
wowFactor: Medical shock isn't one thing — it comes in four distinct varieties, each with a different cause and treatment strategy, all leading to the same life-threatening collapse of circulation.

statement: During cardiac arrest, brain cells begin dying within minutes due to lack of oxygen.
quizQuestion: How quickly do brain cells start dying when the heart stops during cardiac arrest?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, when the heart stops during cardiac arrest, blood flow to the brain is reduced and brain cells begin to die within minutes due to lack of oxygen. This is why rapid CPR and defibrillation are critical — every minute without intervention reduces survival odds significantly.
wowFactor: Cardiac arrest gives rescuers a window measured in minutes — not hours — before the brain begins dying from oxygen starvation. CPR is literally buying time.

statement: Smoking dates back to at least 5000 BCE, where it was used in religious ceremonies and by shamans for spiritual purposes — not recreation.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, smoking can be dated to as early as 5000 BCE, originally evolving in association with religious ceremonies, as offerings to deities, in cleansing rituals, or to allow shamans and priests to alter their minds for divination. wowFactor: Humans have been smoking for 7,000 years — but for most of that time it was a sacred shamanic ritual, not a habit. The recreational cigarette is the late-arriving newcomer.

statement: An aneurysm is a balloon-like bulge in a blood vessel wall — as it grows, the risk of rupture and uncontrolled bleeding increases.
quizQuestion: What happens to an artery-wall weakness's risk of bursting as its size increases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as an aneurysm increases in size, the risk of rupture increases, which could lead to uncontrolled bleeding. Particularly dangerous aneurysms include those in the brain's circle of Willis, the thoracic aorta, and the abdominal aorta.
wowFactor: An aneurysm is essentially a ticking balloon inside your blood vessel — the larger it grows, the more likely it will suddenly burst, causing life-threatening bleeding.

statement: The muscular system has three types of muscle: skeletal (voluntary movement), smooth (organ walls), and cardiac (heart) — and the heart muscle can work autonomously without nervous input.
quizQuestion: Which process in the human body can operate completely without signals from the nervous system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the muscular systems in vertebrates are controlled through the nervous system, but some muscles — specifically cardiac muscle — can be completely autonomous. The heart beats on its own intrinsic electrical system even when disconnected from the nervous system.
wowFactor: Your heart muscle doesn't need your nervous system to beat — it has its own built-in electrical pacemaker that fires independently, which is why it can beat outside the body.

variants.question: True or false: the muscular and skeletal systems together form the musculoskeletal system.
What are the three types of muscle in the human muscular system?
Together with the skeletal system, the muscular system forms the ___ system.
Which organ system maintains posture, permits body movement, and circulates blood?

visualDescription: A pixel-art human figure showing three glowing muscle zones — red skeletal bands on limbs, blue smooth muscle in organ walls, and a golden self-pulsing heart — all labeled.

statement: The human brain consumes approximately 60% of blood glucose in fasting, sedentary individuals — making it the body's most glucose-hungry organ.
quizQuestion: What percentage of blood glucose does the human brain consume in a fasting, sedentary person?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the human brain consumes approximately 60% of blood glucose in fasting, sedentary individuals. The body maintains blood glucose at a constant level — about 4 grams in a 70 kg person — and the brain has first priority on that supply.
wowFactor: Your brain is just 2% of your body weight but it hogs 60% of your blood glucose when you're sitting still — it's an energy-guzzling supercomputer running on sugar.

variants.question: True or false: a 70 kg person maintains approximately 4 grams of dissolved glucose in their blood at all times.
Where is blood glucose stored between meals when it is not circulating?
variants.correctAnswer: Liver and skeletal muscle as glycogen

variants.question: Cellular glucose uptake is primarily regulated by ___, a hormone made in the pancreas.
A person hasn't eaten for 12 hours. Which organ is given priority access to the remaining blood glucose?

visualDescription: A pixel-art brain glows electric blue as 60% of sugar molecules flowing through a bloodstream veer toward it, while other organs wait with smaller shares.

statement: Blood tests are commonly performed by drawing blood from a vein in the arm using a hypodermic needle, or via a simple fingerprick.
quizQuestion: What are the two main ways blood is collected for a blood test?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, blood is usually extracted from a vein in the arm with a hypodermic needle, or via fingerprick for smaller samples. Multiple tests are commonly grouped into panels — like a basic metabolic panel or complete blood count — so a single draw can reveal many physiological states at once.
wowFactor: One small arm poke can reveal your blood sugar, cholesterol, kidney function, and hundreds of other health markers — all from the same tiny vial.

variants.question: True or false: blood tests can be used to detect drug use in addition to disease.
What is a complete blood count an example of?
Multiple blood tests grouped together are called a blood ___ or blood work.
A doctor orders a glucose test and a cholesterol test together. What is this combination called?

visualDescription: A gloved hand holds a thin needle over a bent arm, a tiny vial of crimson blood filling below, clinical white lighting, pixel art style.

statement: Blood tests are used not only for health diagnostics but also in drug tests to detect drug abuse.
quizQuestion: Besides disease detection, what else are blood tests routinely used for?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, blood tests are used in drug tests to detect drug abuse, in addition to their use for determining physiological and biochemical states such as disease, mineral content, and organ function.
wowFactor: The same blood draw that checks if your liver is healthy can also reveal what substances you've taken in the past days — one vial, many secrets.

variants.question: True or false: blood tests can determine how well pharmaceutical drugs are working.
What type of blood panel checks common health markers like glucose and electrolytes?
Blood tests can reveal pharmaceutical drug effectiveness and ___ function.
An employer needs to verify a job applicant hasn't used illicit substances. Which test could provide this from blood?

sensitivityNote: Mentions drug abuse in clinical context — appropriate for teen+
visualDescription: A lab technician examines two glowing vials side by side under a microscope, one labeled health, one labeled toxins, pixel art.

statement: A root canal is not just a dental procedure — it is the naturally occurring hollow space inside a tooth root that contains nerves and blood vessels.
quizQuestion: What is a root canal actually referring to in tooth anatomy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a root canal is the naturally occurring anatomic space within the root of a tooth. It consists of the pulp chamber within the crown, the main canals, and intricate anatomical branches — the term only became associated with the dental procedure because that procedure treats this space.
wowFactor: Most people think a 'root canal' is just a dreaded dental procedure — but it's actually the name of a real part of your tooth that's been there all along!

variants.question: True or false: the root canal includes the pulp chamber within the coronal part of the tooth.
Which tooth structure contains the main canals and intricate anatomical branches?
The root canal consists of the pulp chamber, the main canal, and intricate anatomical ___.
A dentist says your tooth's inner space is infected. What anatomical structure are they referring to?

visualDescription: A cross-section of a molar glowing from within, red and purple canals branching like rivers through ivory white tooth structure, pixel art.

statement: No artificial liver has been developed for long-term replacement — as of 2018, transplantation remains the only option for complete liver failure.
quizQuestion: As of 2018, what is the only option for complete failure?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, it is not known how to compensate for the absence of liver function long-term, and as of 2018, liver transplantation is the only option for complete liver failure. Liver dialysis techniques can be used short-term, but no artificial liver exists for permanent replacement.
wowFactor: Despite centuries of medical progress, we still have no artificial liver — your liver is so complex that if it fails completely, the only fix is getting someone else's.

variants.question: True or false: liver dialysis can be used in the short term to support liver function.
Which organ's complete failure currently has transplantation as the only long-term treatment?
Liver dialysis can support function ___ term, but not long term.
A patient's liver has completely failed. As of 2018, what is the only long-term medical option?

sensitivityNote: Discusses organ failure — clinically informative, not distressing
visualDescription: A surgeon in green scrubs cradles a glowing liver over a pixelated operating table under bright lights, a clock on the wall showing urgency.

statement: The pyloric sphincter controls the passage of partially digested food — called chyme — from the stomach into the duodenum.
quizQuestion: What do you call the partly digested food that exits the stomach into the duodenum?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the pyloric sphincter controls the passage of partially digested food, called chyme, from the stomach into the duodenum — the first and shortest part of the small intestine. After chyme enters the duodenum, peristalsis takes over to move it through the rest of the intestines.
wowFactor: Once your stomach finishes with food, it ships the mashed-up slurry — called chyme — through a trapdoor valve into your small intestine. Your gut has actual valves!

variants.question: True or false: the pyloric sphincter is a valve between the stomach and the duodenum.
Which muscle valve controls food passage from stomach to small intestine?
After leaving the stomach, chyme enters the ___, the first part of the small intestine.
A patient's valve between stomach and small intestine is blocked. Which valve is it?

visualDescription: A cross-section stomach in warm reds and pinks, a tiny valve door opening to release a swirling purple-grey liquid into a glowing tube beyond, pixel art.

statement: The enzyme orotidine 5'-phosphate decarboxylase speeds up a reaction that would otherwise take millions of years — completing it in milliseconds.
quizQuestion: One enzyme accelerates a reaction from millions of years to milliseconds. Which enzyme?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, orotidine 5'-phosphate decarboxylase accelerates a reaction that would otherwise take millions of years to occur — completing it in milliseconds. This is a striking example of how enzymes increase reaction rates, often by factors of millions, by lowering activation energy.
wowFactor: Without enzymes in your cells, some chemical reactions would take millions of years — your body finishes them in milliseconds. Biology is genuinely miraculous.

variants.question: True or false: enzymes are consumed in the chemical reactions they catalyze.
Enzymes increase reaction rates by lowering what?
Enzymes are biological macromolecules, usually a ___, that act as catalysts.
A chemical reaction would naturally take millions of years. An enzyme completes it in milliseconds. What has the enzyme changed?

visualDescription: A tiny glowing protein molecule spinning in a cell, two chemical shapes snapping together in a flash of energy — a clock behind it shows millions of years collapsing to zero, pixel art.

statement: Enzymes are known to catalyze over 5,000 types of biochemical reactions in living organisms.
quizQuestion: How many types of biochemical reactions can enzymes catalyze?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, enzymes are known to catalyze over 5,000 types of biochemical reactions. They are essential to virtually all metabolic processes and give living organisms the ability to perform chemistry that would otherwise be impossibly slow at body temperature.
wowFactor: Your body runs on over 5,000 different enzyme-powered chemical reactions happening simultaneously — you are basically a walking chemistry lab.

variants.question: True or false: enzymes affect the overall equilibrium of a chemical reaction.
What group of molecules catalyzes over 5,000 types of biochemical reactions?
Enzyme activity can be diminished by substances called ___.
A poison works by blocking a specific molecular machine in your cells that speeds up chemical reactions. What is it blocking?

visualDescription: A colorful grid of molecular lock-and-key pairs glowing in a cell interior, each pair a different color representing a different reaction, pixel art style.

statement: Metabolism has two opposing processes: catabolism breaks compounds down and releases energy, while anabolism builds compounds up and consumes energy.
quizQuestion: Which metabolic process releases energy by breaking down compounds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, catabolism breaks down compounds — for example, glucose to pyruvate by cellular respiration — and releases energy, while anabolism builds up compounds like proteins and nucleic acids and consumes energy. Together, these two opposing processes form the foundation of metabolism.
wowFactor: Every moment your body is simultaneously demolishing molecules for fuel AND constructing new ones for growth — it's a non-stop construction zone and demolition crew working side by side.

variants.question: True or false: anabolism releases energy by breaking down compounds.
Which metabolic process consumes energy to build up compounds like proteins?
Metabolic reactions are either anabolic (building up) or ___ (breaking down).
When you exercise, your muscles break down glucose to release energy. Which metabolic process is this?

visualDescription: Two glowing arrows — one red pointing downward (breaking apart molecules) and one blue pointing upward (assembling molecules) — crossing inside a cell, pixel art.

statement: The citric acid cycle intermediates are found in every known living organism, from bacteria like E. coli to elephants.
quizQuestion: Which metabolic pathway's intermediates are found in all known organisms from bacteria to elephants?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the set of carboxylic acids that are the intermediates in the citric acid cycle are present in all known organisms — found in species as diverse as the unicellular bacterium E. coli and multicellular organisms like elephants. This universal conservation suggests these pathways appeared very early in evolutionary history.
wowFactor: You share the same core energy-making chemical pathway with a bacterium, an oak tree, and an elephant — the citric acid cycle is a 3-billion-year-old recipe that life never stopped using.

variants.question: True or false: the basic metabolic pathways are remarkably similar across vastly different species.
Which bacteria is cited as sharing metabolic pathways with large animals like elephants?
The citric acid cycle is also known as the ___ cycle, after the biochemist who discovered it.
A scientist finds the same chemical intermediates in a bacterium and an elephant. Which metabolic pathway explains this universal conservation?

visualDescription: A tiny bacterium and a giant elephant standing side by side, both with a glowing spinning wheel of chemical reactions in their centers, pixel art.

statement: Oral rehydration solution and zinc tablets have been estimated to have saved 50 million children from diarrhea-related deaths in the past 25 years.
quizQuestion: How many children have ORS and zinc tablets been estimated to have saved in 25 years?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, oral rehydration solution (ORS) — clean water with modest amounts of salts and sugar — combined with zinc tablets, has been estimated to have saved 50 million children over the past 25 years. These simple, cheap treatments address the primary danger of diarrhea: dehydration.
wowFactor: A mix of water, a pinch of salt, and a bit of sugar — oral rehydration solution may be the most lives-saved-per-dollar intervention in medical history, saving 50 million children.

variants.question: True or false: the main danger of severe diarrhea is dehydration, not the infection itself.
What simple solution is the treatment of choice for diarrhea-related dehydration?
ORS is clean water with modest amounts of salts and ___.
A child in a developing country has severe diarrhea but no hospital nearby. What is the recommended first treatment?

visualDescription: A small child drinking from a bright cup in a rural setting, a health worker smiling nearby holding a packet of salts, warm golden pixel art light.

statement: Digestion begins in the mouth: saliva contains an enzyme called salivary amylase that starts breaking down starch before you even swallow.
quizQuestion: Which enzyme in saliva begins digesting starch in the mouth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, saliva contains salivary amylase, an enzyme that starts the digestion of starch in the food while still in the mouth. About 30% of starch is hydrolyzed into disaccharide right there in the oral cavity — meaning digestion is already well underway before you swallow.
wowFactor: Digestion starts the moment food touches your tongue — your saliva is already dissolving starch before you've swallowed a single bite.

variants.question: True or false: about 30% of starch is digested in the mouth before swallowing.
Which substance contains salivary amylase that begins starch digestion?
After chewing and starch digestion, food forms a round mass called a ___.
You chew a piece of bread and it begins tasting slightly sweet. Which enzyme is responsible?

visualDescription: A close-up mouth with a glowing blue enzyme dissolving a bread crumb on the tongue, tiny starch molecules fragmenting into sugars, pixel art.

statement: The small intestine is about 6.5 metres (21 feet) long — yet it's called 'small' because it is narrower in diameter than the large intestine.
quizQuestion: Why is the small intestine called 'small' despite being about 6.5 metres long?
distractors: It absorbs less than the large intestine
It was discovered after the large intestine
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the small intestine is about 6.5 metres (21 feet) long and folds many times to fit in the abdomen. Although it is longer than the large intestine, it is called 'small' because it is narrower in diameter — a common misconception that 'small' refers to length.
wowFactor: The small intestine is 21 feet long — longer than a car — yet it's called 'small' only because it's narrower than the large intestine. Names in anatomy can be deeply misleading.

variants.question: True or false: the small intestine is longer than the large intestine.
Approximately how long is the human small intestine?
The small intestine has three regions: the duodenum, jejunum, and ___.
A student thinks the 'small' intestine must be shorter than the large intestine. What property actually makes it 'small'?

visualDescription: A neatly coiled tube of small intestine stretched out beside a car for scale — glowing pink and looped many times, pixel art style.

statement: Some communities face a 'double burden of malnutrition' — where obesity and undernutrition coexist in the same population.
quizQuestion: What term describes communities where obesity and undernutrition coexist together?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the World Health Organization and The Lancet Commission have identified 'the double burden of malnutrition,' which occurs from the coexistence of overnutrition (overweight and obesity) alongside undernutrition (stunted growth and wasting). This paradox is increasingly seen in developing countries.
wowFactor: In some developing communities, children are both stunted from hunger and obese from poor-quality food — the body starved for nutrients while flooded with empty calories.

variants.question: True or false: overnutrition is a recognized form of malnutrition.
What form of malnutrition can result in stunted growth and wasting?
Malnutrition includes both undernutrition and ___, which can cause obesity.
A Lancet report finds a village with both severely underweight children and obese adults. What phenomenon is this?

sensitivityNote: Discusses poverty and nutritional hardship — informative, non-graphic
visualDescription: Two silhouettes side by side — one thin and gaunt, one overweight — both in the same pixelated village street under a dim sun, showing contrast.

statement: The large intestine is about 1.5 metres long — only one-fifth the length of the entire human gastrointestinal tract.
quizQuestion: What fraction of the total GI tract does the large intestine make up?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the large intestine is about 1.5 metres (5 ft) long in humans, which is approximately one-fifth of the whole length of the human gastrointestinal tract. Despite being the final stage of digestion, it's far shorter than the small intestine that precedes it.
wowFactor: Despite its name, the 'large' intestine is only one-fifth the length of your entire gut — the 'small' intestine does most of the heavy lifting for nearly four-fifths of the journey.

variants.question: True or false: the large intestine is approximately 1.5 metres long in humans.
What is the primary function of the large intestine?
The large intestine stores waste material in the ___ as feces before defecation.
A medical diagram shows the GI tract is 7.5 metres total. How long would the large intestine portion be?

visualDescription: A glowing diagram of the GI tract with the large intestine highlighted in deep red — noticeably shorter than the looping small intestine above it, pixel art.

statement: During swallowing, the epiglottis tilts backward to prevent food from entering the lungs — a reflex that happens automatically every time you eat.
quizQuestion: Which body part tilts backward during swallowing to prevent food from entering the lungs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, during swallowing, the epiglottis tilts backwards to prevent food from going down the larynx and into the lungs. The esophagus then carries food via peristaltic contractions to the stomach — a tube about 25 cm (10 inches) long running behind the trachea and heart.
wowFactor: Every time you swallow, a tiny flap in your throat — the epiglottis — snaps shut in milliseconds to keep food out of your lungs. It's an automatic trapdoor you never consciously control.

variants.question: True or false: the esophagus is about 25 cm long in adult humans.
Which tube carries food from the pharynx to the stomach via peristaltic contractions?
The esophagus passes through the ___ before emptying into the stomach.
Someone 'went down the wrong pipe' — food entered the airway. Which automatic reflex failed to prevent this?

visualDescription: A glowing cross-section of a throat at the moment of swallowing, a small leaf-shaped flap (epiglottis) tilting shut over the airway, protecting the lungs below, pixel art.

statement: The esophagus is a fibromuscular tube about 25 cm (10 inches) long that travels behind the trachea and heart to connect the throat to the stomach.
quizQuestion: Approximately how long is the human esophagus in adult humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the esophagus is about 25 cm (10 in) long in adult humans. It travels behind the trachea and heart, passes through the diaphragm, and empties into the uppermost region of the stomach. It is described as a fibromuscular tube — a blend of fibrous and muscle tissue.
wowFactor: The esophagus snakes behind your heart and trachea for about 10 inches — and surgeons say it's one of the hardest organs to access precisely because of where it's sandwiched.

variants.question: True or false: the esophagus passes through the diaphragm on its way to the stomach.
What mechanism does the esophagus use to move food toward the stomach?
The lower sphincter of the esophagus helps prevent ___ of acidic stomach content.
Which organ connects the pharynx to the stomach in vertebrates?

visualDescription: A glowing pink tube threading behind a stylized pixel art heart and windpipe, bending downward through a diaphragm to reach the stomach.

statement: In iodine-deficient regions, hypothyroidism is the leading cause of preventable intellectual disability in children.
quizQuestion: In iodine-deficient areas, what condition is the leading cause of preventable intellectual disability in children?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in iodine-deficient regions, hypothyroidism due to iodine deficiency is the leading cause of preventable intellectual disability in children. The thyroid gland requires iodine to produce thyroid hormones, which are essential for brain development and growth — making iodized salt one of the highest-impact public health interventions in history.
wowFactor: Adding a tiny amount of iodine to table salt prevents the most common cause of preventable intellectual disability in the world — one cheap kitchen staple protects millions of developing brains.

variants.question: True or false: the thyroid gland is butterfly-shaped and located in the neck.
Which mineral deficiency most commonly causes hypothyroidism worldwide?
The thyroid gland secretes three hormones: T3, T4, and the peptide hormone ___.
A remote region reports high rates of cognitive impairment in children. Iodine is found to be low. What condition is likely to blame?

sensitivityNote: Discusses intellectual disability in an educational, non-stigmatizing context
visualDescription: A glowing butterfly-shaped thyroid in the neck, with a small iodine crystal and salt shaker beside it, contrasting a healthy and stunted brain silhouette, pixel art.

statement: The thyroid gland is butterfly-shaped and secretes three hormones: T3, T4, and calcitonin.
quizQuestion: How many does the thyroid gland secrete?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the thyroid gland secretes three hormones: triiodothyronine (T3) and thyroxine (T4) — which influence metabolic rate, protein synthesis, and growth in children — and calcitonin, a peptide hormone that plays a role in calcium homeostasis. The thyroid is located in the neck below the Adam's apple.
wowFactor: That butterfly-shaped lump in your throat controls your metabolism, your growth, and even your blood calcium — three massive body systems, all from one gland.

variants.question: True or false: calcitonin plays a role in calcium homeostasis in the body.
Which autoimmune disorder is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism?
The thyroid is regulated by thyroid-stimulating hormone secreted from the ___ pituitary gland.
Which gland's secretion of T3 and T4 influences metabolic rate throughout the body?

visualDescription: A glowing butterfly-shaped gland in a neck cross-section, three colored hormone streams flowing out from it — one regulating metabolism, one controlling calcium, pixel art.

statement: In children, rotavirus is the most common cause of severe gastroenteritis, despite the condition being commonly mislabeled 'stomach flu.'
quizQuestion: What is the most common cause of severe gastroenteritis in children?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in children rotavirus is the most common cause of severe gastroenteritis, while in adults norovirus and Campylobacter are more common causes. Despite often being called 'stomach flu,' gastroenteritis is not related to influenza.
wowFactor: What most people call 'stomach flu' has nothing to do with flu — and the villain behind children's worst cases is rotavirus, a tiny pathogen that can cause life-threatening dehydration in days.

variants.question: True or false: gastroenteritis is related to the influenza virus.
What are the two most common causes of gastroenteritis in adults?
A rotavirus ___ is recommended as prevention for children against gastroenteritis.
A toddler has severe vomiting and diarrhea during winter. Which pathogen is the most likely cause?

visualDescription: A microscopic rotavirus particle glowing red and spiky against a dark background, a small child silhouette faintly visible behind a hospital curtain, pixel art.

statement: In 2015, there were 2 billion cases of gastroenteritis globally, resulting in 1.3 million deaths.
quizQuestion: How many cases of gastroenteritis occurred worldwide in 2015?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2015 there were two billion cases of gastroenteritis, resulting in 1.3 million deaths globally. Children and those in the developing world are most severely affected — in developing countries, children under two years old frequently get six or more infections per year.
wowFactor: Gastroenteritis — often dismissed as an upset stomach — struck 2 billion people in a single year. It's one of the most common diseases on Earth.

variants.question: True or false: children in developing countries can get gastroenteritis six or more times per year.
How many deaths did gastroenteritis cause globally in 2015?
Gastroenteritis is also known as infectious ___.
Which disease caused 2 billion cases and 1.3 million deaths globally in 2015?

visualDescription: A global map with glowing red dots spread across every continent, most concentrated in developing regions — representing billions of gastroenteritis cases, pixel art.

statement: Hepatitis D can only infect people who are already infected with Hepatitis B — making it uniquely dependent on another virus to function.
quizQuestion: Which liver disease type can only infect people already infected with the B variant?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hepatitis D can only infect people already infected with Hepatitis B. This makes Hepatitis D a satellite virus — it requires the surface proteins of Hepatitis B to replicate and infect cells. Getting vaccinated against Hepatitis B therefore automatically protects against Hepatitis D as well.
wowFactor: Hepatitis D is a virus that can't even function on its own — it needs Hepatitis B as a host virus first. One vaccine takes out two viruses at once.

variants.question: True or false: the Hepatitis B vaccine also protects against Hepatitis D.
How is Hepatitis B most commonly transmitted?
Hepatitis A and E are mainly spread by contaminated food and ___.
A patient has both Hepatitis B and Hepatitis D co-infection. Why can't Hepatitis D exist without Hepatitis B?
variants.correctAnswer: It requires Hepatitis B to replicate
distractors: D is a subtype of B

sensitivityNote: Discusses sexually transmitted disease in clinical educational context
visualDescription: Two virus particles side by side — a large Hepatitis B particle and a smaller Hepatitis D particle clinging to it — glowing against a dark microscopic background, pixel art.

statement: Hepatitis can cause jaundice — a yellow discoloration of the skin and the whites of the eyes — as bile pigments build up in the blood.
quizQuestion: What name is given to the yellow discoloration of skin and eyes caused by liver inflammation?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hepatitis — inflammation of the liver tissue — can cause yellow discoloration of the skin and whites of the eyes, known as jaundice, among other symptoms like poor appetite, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Chronic hepatitis may eventually progress to cirrhosis, liver failure, or liver cancer.
wowFactor: When the liver is under attack, it can literally turn your eyes yellow — jaundice is your body's visible distress signal that the chemical factory is in trouble.

variants.question: True or false: hepatitis is classified as acute if it resolves within six months.
Which hepatitis type is most commonly spread through infected blood, such as during needle sharing?
Chronic hepatitis may progress to scarring of the liver, a condition called ___.
A patient's eyes have turned yellow after weeks of fatigue and abdominal pain. What symptom is this and what organ is likely inflamed?

visualDescription: A pixelated face with yellowed eyes and skin, a glowing red inflamed liver visible in a body silhouette behind, clinical blue hospital lighting.

statement: The entire human gastrointestinal tract is about 9 metres (30 feet) long at autopsy — nearly the length of a school bus.
quizQuestion: How long is the entire human gastrointestinal tract at autopsy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the whole human GI tract is about nine metres (30 feet) long at autopsy. It is considerably shorter in the living body because the intestines, which are tubes of smooth muscle tissue, maintain constant muscle tone in a halfway-tense state — they relax after death, lengthening significantly.
wowFactor: Your gut is 30 feet long — nearly the length of a school bus — but it's coiled and contracted inside you. It actually gets longer after you die as the muscles relax.

variants.question: True or false: the GI tract is longer in a living person than at autopsy.
How many different strains of bacteria, archaea, viruses, and eukaryotes make up the human gut microbiota?
The GI tract is divided into the upper and ___ gastrointestinal tracts.
Which body system runs from the mouth to the anus and is approximately 9 metres long?

visualDescription: A glowing uncoiled GI tract stretched out beside a school bus silhouette showing how they're the same length, warm anatomical illustration in pixel art.

statement: The inner ear's semicircular canals enable balance and eye tracking during movement — making the ear an organ of both hearing and balance.
quizQuestion: Which inner ear structures enable balance and eye tracking during movement?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the semicircular canals in the inner ear enable balance and eye tracking during movement. The utricle and saccule enable balance when stationary, while the cochlea enables hearing. The ear therefore serves two distinct senses — hearing and balance — via three separate structures in the inner ear.
wowFactor: Your ears don't just hear — they contain tiny fluid-filled loops that track every tilt and spin of your head, running your sense of balance 24 hours a day without you noticing.

variants.question: True or false: the cochlea in the inner ear is responsible for enabling hearing.
Which inner ear structures enable balance when the body is stationary (not moving)?
The outer ear consists of the auricle and the ear ___.
After a spinning ride, someone feels dizzy. Which inner ear structures detected all that rotation?

visualDescription: A cross-section of the human inner ear with glowing blue fluid-filled semicircular rings curving around a snail-shell cochlea, pixel art anatomy diagram.

statement: A protruding nose completely separate from the mouth is a characteristic found only in therian mammals — no other vertebrate has this anatomy.
quizQuestion: Which group of animals is the only one with a protruding nose completely separate from the mouth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the protruding nose that is completely separate from the mouth part is a characteristic found only in therian mammals. It is theorized that this unique mammalian nose evolved from the anterior part of the upper jaw of reptilian-like ancestors called synapsids.
wowFactor: The classic 'nose' — that bump sticking out separately from your mouth — is uniquely mammalian. Reptiles, birds, and fish all have nostrils, but none have a protruding nose distinct from their mouth like we do.

variants.question: True or false: the human protruding nose is theorized to have evolved from ancestral reptile-like jaw structure.
Besides smell, what other function does the nose serve in humans?
The nasal cavity connects to the paranasal ___, which buffer pressure and humidify air.
A scientist notes that lizards have nostril openings but no separate nose structure. Which animal group has a true protruding nose?

visualDescription: Side-by-side pixel art profiles — a lizard with flat nostril slits and a human with a protruding nose — highlighted to show the unique mammalian difference.

statement: The nasal cavity leads into the pharynx, which acts as a switch valve connecting both the respiratory and digestive systems.
quizQuestion: What body part acts as a 'switch valve' connecting the respiratory and digestive systems?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, from the nasal cavity the nostrils continue into the pharynx, described as a 'switch track valve' connecting the respiratory and digestive systems. This is why swallowing and breathing must be carefully coordinated — both pathways pass through this shared junction.
wowFactor: Every time you breathe and eat, they share the same junction in your throat — the pharynx acts like a biological railway switch, routing air to the lungs and food to the stomach.

variants.question: True or false: the nose serves as an alternative respiratory passage especially useful for infants during suckling.
What is the term for the pair of external openings of the nose?
The nose contains olfactory ___ which detects airborne chemical compounds.
Food and air both pass through the same throat junction before being routed to different systems. What is this shared junction?

visualDescription: A glowing throat cross-section with two glowing arrows — one blue going to lungs, one orange going to stomach — splitting at a central switch point labeled pharynx, pixel art.

statement: The four primary tissue types in the human body are epithelial, connective, muscular, and nervous.
quizQuestion: Which four primary tissue types make up the human body?
explanation: All organs and structures in the body are built from combinations of just four primary tissue types: epithelial (covering surfaces), connective (supporting/binding), muscular (movement), and nervous (signaling).
wowFactor: Every structure in the human body — from the brain to the big toe — is assembled from just these four fundamental tissue categories.

variants.question: How many primary tissue types exist in the human body?
Vascular tissue is one of the four primary tissue types in the human body.
Which tissue type is responsible for signal transmission in the body?
Which primary tissue type provides movement?

visualDescription: Colorful microscopy grid showing four tissue panels side by side: pink epithelial sheet, fibrous connective tissue, striated muscle fibers, and branching neurons.

statement: Epithelial tissue is avascular, meaning it contains no blood vessels and receives nutrients by diffusion from underlying connective tissue.
quizQuestion: Why is epithelial tissue described as avascular?
explanation: Epithelial tissue lines body surfaces and cavities but has no direct blood supply. It relies on diffusion of oxygen and nutrients from the vascularized connective tissue beneath it, separated by a basement membrane.
wowFactor: The skin surface you touch every day has zero blood vessels — nutrients must diffuse up from layers below.

variants.question: Epithelial tissue contains its own blood vessels to supply nutrients.
What structure separates epithelial tissue from underlying connective tissue?
How does epithelial tissue receive its nutrients?
The basement membrane anchors epithelial tissue to underlying connective tissue.

visualDescription: Cross-section diagram showing a layered epithelial sheet above a dashed basement membrane line, with red capillaries visible only in the connective tissue layer beneath.

statement: Connective tissue is the most abundant tissue type in the body and includes bone, cartilage, blood, and adipose tissue.
quizQuestion: Which tissue type is most abundant in the human body?
explanation: Connective tissue is the most widespread tissue type, forming a diverse family that includes dense bone, flexible cartilage, liquid blood, and fat-storing adipose tissue. It binds, supports, and separates other tissues throughout the body.
wowFactor: Blood is technically a connective tissue — the same category as bone and fat — just in liquid form.

variants.question: Blood is classified as a type of connective tissue.
Which of the following is NOT a type of connective tissue?
Adipose tissue belongs to which primary tissue category?
Connective tissue is less abundant than muscular tissue in the human body.

visualDescription: Four-panel collage showing connective tissue diversity: cross-section of bone, translucent cartilage, red blood cells in plasma, and yellow adipose fat cells.

statement: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain is the most common histological stain; hematoxylin stains nuclei blue and eosin stains cytoplasm pink.
quizQuestion: In H&E staining, what color does hematoxylin stain cell nuclei?
explanation: H&E is the gold standard histological stain. Hematoxylin, a basic dye, binds to acidic structures like DNA in nuclei, producing a blue-purple color. Eosin, an acidic dye, binds to basic proteins in the cytoplasm, staining them pink.
wowFactor: Pathologists can diagnose cancer, infection, and dozens of diseases just by interpreting the blue and pink patterns under a microscope.

variants.question: What color does eosin stain the cytoplasm in H&E histology?
H&E staining is the most commonly used histological technique.
What does the 'H' stand for in H&E staining?
In H&E staining, eosin stains cell nuclei blue.

visualDescription: Microscope slide showing tissue section with deep blue-purple nuclei scattered among pink-stained cytoplasm and extracellular matrix, classic H&E appearance.

statement: Red blood cells are the only mature mammalian cells that lack a nucleus, allowing more room for hemoglobin.
quizQuestion: Which mammalian cells are unique in having no nucleus at maturity?
explanation: Mature mammalian red blood cells (erythrocytes) eject their nucleus and organelles during development, maximizing space for hemoglobin to carry oxygen. This makes them the only anucleate cells in the mammalian body.
wowFactor: Your blood cells sacrifice their DNA to become more efficient oxygen taxis — a cellular trade-off perfected over millions of years of evolution.

variants.question: Mature human red blood cells contain a nucleus.
Why do red blood cells lack a nucleus?
What is the scientific name for red blood cells?
Platelets are complete cells with nuclei, unlike red blood cells.

visualDescription: Electron microscopy view of biconcave red blood cells — smooth, dimpled discs — contrasted with a white blood cell with a visible lobed nucleus nearby.

statement: The Golgi apparatus packages and ships proteins to their destinations; it was discovered by Camillo Golgi in 1898.
quizQuestion: What organelle packages proteins for transport, discovered by Camillo Golgi?
explanation: The Golgi apparatus acts as the cell's post office, receiving proteins from the endoplasmic reticulum, modifying them, and packaging them into vesicles for delivery to specific destinations inside or outside the cell.
wowFactor: Camillo Golgi won the 1906 Nobel Prize in Physiology — but initially many scientists didn't believe the Golgi apparatus was real, thinking it was a microscopy artifact.

variants.question: When did Camillo Golgi discover the Golgi apparatus?
The Golgi apparatus synthesizes proteins from amino acids.
What does the Golgi apparatus receive proteins from before packaging them?
The Golgi apparatus is sometimes called the cell's post office.

visualDescription: Electron micrograph of the Golgi apparatus: a stack of flattened membrane cisternae with vesicles budding off the edges, resembling a layered pancake stack.

statement: Mitochondria contain their own DNA and are inherited exclusively from the mother, not the father.
quizQuestion: From which parent do humans inherit mitochondrial DNA?
explanation: Mitochondria have their own circular DNA separate from nuclear DNA, a relic of their ancient bacterial ancestors. Because sperm contribute almost no cytoplasm (where mitochondria live) at fertilization, mitochondrial DNA is passed down only through the mother.
wowFactor: Scientists can trace human maternal lineages back thousands of generations using mitochondrial DNA — it was used to identify the remains of Tsar Nicholas II of Russia.

variants.question: Mitochondria contain their own DNA separate from nuclear DNA.
Why is mitochondrial DNA inherited only from mothers?
variants.correctAnswer: Sperm contribute almost no cytoplasm at fertilization

variants.question: Mitochondrial DNA is most similar to DNA from which organism?
Paternal mitochondrial DNA is equally inherited alongside maternal mitochondrial DNA.

visualDescription: Diagram of a mitochondrion cut open to reveal its inner membrane folds, with a circular mitochondrial DNA strand highlighted in blue floating in the matrix.

statement: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells capable of self-renewal and differentiation into specialized cell types.
quizQuestion: What unique ability allows stem cells to become many different specialized cell types?
explanation: Stem cells retain the capacity for both self-renewal (dividing to produce more stem cells) and differentiation (developing into specialized cell types like neurons, muscle cells, or red blood cells). This makes them central to development, tissue repair, and regenerative medicine.
wowFactor: A single fertilized egg — a stem cell — gives rise to the 37 trillion specialized cells that make up a complete human body.

variants.question: Stem cells are fully specialized cells that cannot change their function.
Which term describes a stem cell's ability to self-renew and form copies of itself?
Which type of stem cell can differentiate into any cell type including placental cells?
Stem cells can only be found in embryos, not in adult tissues.

visualDescription: Branching tree diagram showing a single stem cell dividing and differentiating into diverse specialized cells: neurons, red blood cells, muscle fibers, and liver cells.

statement: Neurons are the longest cells in the human body, with some axons reaching up to 1 meter in length.
quizQuestion: Which cells are the longest in the human body, reaching up to 1 meter?
explanation: Motor neurons extending from the spinal cord to the tips of the toes can be up to 1 meter long, making them the longest cells in the body. The single axon of one neuron can stretch this entire distance while the cell body remains in the spinal cord.
wowFactor: A single nerve cell in your leg is long enough to stretch from your lower back to your big toe — one cell, one meter.

variants.question: Neurons are the longest cells in the human body.
What part of a neuron can extend up to 1 meter in length?
Where does the cell body of a 1-meter-long motor neuron typically reside?
Muscle fibers are longer than neurons in the human body.

visualDescription: Full-body silhouette showing a single neuron's axon drawn as a long continuous line running from the lumbar spinal cord all the way down the leg to the foot.

statement: Platelets are cell fragments (not complete cells) derived from megakaryocytes; normal count is 150,000–400,000 per microliter of blood.
quizQuestion: Platelets are cell fragments derived from which large bone marrow cell?
explanation: Platelets (thrombocytes) are small anucleate cell fragments shed from large bone marrow cells called megakaryocytes. Each megakaryocyte produces thousands of platelets. Normal platelet count is 150,000–400,000 per microliter, and they are essential for blood clotting.
wowFactor: Platelets aren't cells at all — they're pieces of a larger cell that shatter apart in bone marrow to create thousands of tiny clotting agents.

variants.question: Platelets are complete cells with their own nuclei.
What is the normal platelet count range per microliter of blood?
What is the primary function of platelets in the body?
Platelets are produced in the bone marrow.

visualDescription: Microscopic view of a giant megakaryocyte cell in bone marrow with dozens of tiny platelet fragments budding off its surface into surrounding blood vessels.

statement: The basement membrane is a thin extracellular matrix layer that anchors epithelial cells to underlying connective tissue.
quizQuestion: What thin layer anchors epithelial cells to the underlying connective tissue?
explanation: The basement membrane is a specialized extracellular matrix layer composed mainly of collagen IV, laminin, and proteoglycans. It physically anchors epithelial layers to underlying connective tissue and also acts as a selective filtration barrier and signaling scaffold.
wowFactor: The basement membrane in the kidney glomerulus filters your entire blood volume about 60 times per day, separating waste from nutrients with nanometer precision.

variants.question: The basement membrane is a component of the cell's plasma membrane.
Which protein is a major structural component of the basement membrane?
Besides anchoring epithelium, what other function does the basement membrane serve?
The basement membrane is made of extracellular matrix proteins.
